docs.title: What This Platform Is
docs.text:
This is a static, early-stage training site built to support better readiness conversations. It is not an accredited school, seminary, mission board, sending agency, full LMS, or finished certification ecosystem.

The goal is simple: provide clear course materials that help learners prepare with biblical faithfulness, cross-cultural humility, accountability, safety, and practical care.

Missionaries and families

Churches and mentors

Sponsors and partners

docs.title: Training Approach
docs.text: Formation before activity. Readiness begins with Scripture, prayer, character, worship, and obedience. Humility before strategy. Cross-cultural service should honor local churches, local leaders, and local communities. Care before crisis. Preparation includes family safety, child protection, support systems, and appropriate boundaries. Practical next steps. Courses include lessons, reflection questions, knowledge checks, and assignments for discussion.

docs.title: Learning For Varied Contexts
docs.text:
Missionary readiness should prepare people to listen and serve across rural and urban settings, among communities marked by simplicity, tradition, modern growth, and material prosperity. Training should help learners avoid assumptions and enter each place with humility.

Rural preparation: listening, patience, and respect for local rhythms. Urban preparation: responsibility, complexity, and everyday professional life. Community preparation: dignity, local relationships, and ordinary service.

Images are illustrative editorial artwork used to support reflective reading. They do not depict platform participants, partner organizations, or sending endorsements.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness Institute is an emerging online training platform focused on missionary readiness. It exists to help independent missionaries, church members, mission-minded families, local churches, and sponsoring partners prepare for cross-cultural service with humility, accountability, and practical care.

The platform is not a university, seminary, accredited institution, or sending authority. It is a practical training resource designed to support thoughtful preparation, local church involvement, and responsible mission practice.

docs.title: Why This Platform Exists
docs.text: Readiness begins with respect for real people, local communities, and the ordinary conditions of service.   Missionary service often begins with sincere desire, but desire alone is not enough. Workers and supporters need space to think carefully about spiritual formation, cultural humility, safety, accountability, family realities, local church relationships, and practical preparation.  This site is being developed to provide that kind of starting point: clear public resources, course outlines, reflection questions, and practical assignments that can support better conversations before service begins.

docs.title: Who This Is For
docs.text:
• Independent missionaries preparing for service.
  • Church members discerning missionary involvement.
  • Mission-minded families considering cross-cultural work.
  • Local churches training or sending workers.
  • Sponsors and ministry partners who want to support workers responsibly.
  • Small organizations that need clear readiness guidance.

docs.title: What We Emphasize
docs.text:
Readiness is more than enthusiasm. It includes biblical faithfulness, spiritual maturity, cross-cultural respect, family and child safety, legal compliance, accountability, teachability, and practical service.

Courses and public pages are written to help learners ask better questions, prepare wisely, and serve in ways that honor local churches, local leaders, and the communities they hope to bless. The goal is not to create unnecessary barriers, but to encourage sober preparation and faithful service.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text:
This site is still developing. Content should be treated as introductory training material, not as official accreditation, legal advice, professional counsel, or a replacement for church leadership, pastoral care, qualified professional guidance, or field-specific requirements.

As the platform matures, course pages may be expanded with additional lessons, facilitator notes, and certificates of completion. A certificate of completion would only indicate participation in course material; it would not be an accredited certification or approval for missionary service.

docs.title: Mission & Vision
docs.text: Missionary Readiness Institute is an emerging training platform for people preparing to serve, send, sponsor, or support cross-cultural mission work. This page summarizes the purpose and commitments shaping the platform.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Missionary Readiness Institute provides practical, biblically faithful, and culturally respectful training that helps missionaries, churches, families, and sponsors prepare for responsible cross-cultural service.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
We want mission-minded people to enter service with humility, preparation, accountability, and love for the communities they serve. Readiness should strengthen local churches, protect vulnerable people, respect local leaders, and help workers serve with wisdom rather than impulse.

The vision is not to replace churches, mission boards, sending agencies, pastoral counsel, or field-specific preparation. The platform exists to support better readiness conversations and provide practical training resources that can be used by individuals, families, churches, sponsors, and small organizations.

Readiness is strongest when preparation happens with humility, shared counsel, and respect across cultures and generations.

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
The platform is being developed for:

  • Independent missionaries preparing for service.
  • Church members discerning missionary involvement.
  • Mission-minded families preparing together.
  • Local churches training or sending workers.
  • Sponsors and ministry partners supporting responsibly.
  • Small organizations that need practical readiness guidance.

docs.title: Guiding Commitments
docs.text:
• Biblical faithfulness: Readiness begins with Scripture, prayer, worship, obedience, and character.
  • Cross-cultural humility: Workers enter new contexts as learners, not experts.
  • Local respect: Mission practice should honor local churches, local leaders, and local communities.
  • Safety and care: Preparation should consider family safety, child protection, personal health, and appropriate boundaries.
  • Accountability: Workers should remain connected to responsible churches, mentors, sponsors, and ministry leaders.
  • Practical service: Training should prepare learners for real decisions, field conditions, and ordinary acts of faithful service.

docs.title: What Success Looks Like
docs.text:
Learners should be able to name their next preparation steps, identify support needs and risks, communicate clearly with churches and sponsors, and approach cross-cultural ministry with sober hope and teachable confidence.

Churches and sponsors should be able to use the material to ask better questions, clarify expectations, identify concerns early, and support workers more responsibly.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text:
This is an emerging training platform. It is not an accredited school, seminary, mission board, sending agency, or degree-granting institution.

The platform may eventually support certificates of completion for course participation. A certificate of completion would not be an accredited certification, academic degree, ordination, commissioning, or approval for missionary service.

docs.title: Sponsor Brief
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness Institute is an early-stage online training platform focused on practical missionary readiness. It is being developed to help independent missionaries, church members, mission-minded families, local churches, sponsors, and small organizations prepare for cross-cultural service with humility, accountability, biblical faithfulness, and practical care.

The platform is not a university, seminary, accredited institution, mission board, or sending agency. It is a service-oriented training resource meant to strengthen thoughtful preparation and better conversations before, during, and after missionary service.

Responsible support strengthens learning, accountability, and practical care without replacing local wisdom or personal responsibility.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Many mission-minded people step toward service with sincere desire but limited preparation. Independent missionaries and small sending networks may not have a shared framework for discussing spiritual formation, cross-cultural humility, safety, accountability, family realities, legal compliance, language learning, or long-term care.

Churches and sponsors often want to help but may lack practical tools for asking good questions, identifying risks early, and supporting workers responsibly.

docs.title: The Proposed Solution
docs.text:
The Missionary Readiness Platform provides a clear, public, beginner-friendly training library that can support readiness conversations between workers, churches, sponsors, families, mentors, and ministry leaders.

A minimum viable platform would include:

  • Public pages explaining the purpose, audience, and guiding commitments.
  • Starter policy pages for privacy, terms, acceptable use, copyright, and disclaimers.
  • A course catalog with foundational readiness topics.
  • A reusable course template for consistent course development.
  • Introductory courses with lessons, reflection questions, knowledge checks, practical assignments, facilitator notes, and completion criteria.
  • Clear language about certificates of completion without claiming accreditation.

docs.title: Who It Serves
docs.text:
• Independent missionaries: Practical preparation before serving alone or outside a large agency.
  • Local churches: A framework for discernment, mentoring, sending conversations, and care.
  • Sponsors and private donors: Better questions for responsible support and accountability.
  • Mission-minded families: Shared language for preparation, safety, communication, and expectations.
  • Small organizations: A lightweight training structure that can be reviewed, adapted, and improved.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Good intentions do not remove the need for preparation. Missionary readiness helps workers serve with greater humility, respect local churches and leaders, protect vulnerable people, communicate clearly with supporters, and recognize when qualified professional guidance is needed.

The goal is not to create unnecessary barriers. The goal is sober preparation for faithful service.

docs.title: Immediate Next Steps
docs.text:
The current priority is to develop a credible minimum viable platform:

  1. Polish the public website and policy pages.
  2. Expand the first course, Missionary Readiness 101.
  3. Build out foundational courses in spiritual formation, cross-cultural service, missionary care, language learning, and field readiness.
  4. Invite pastors, missionaries, sponsors, educators, and ministry leaders to review the material.
  5. Identify a simple process for feedback, revisions, and future certificates of completion.

docs.title: Ways Supporters Can Help
docs.text:
Supporters can help in practical, non-pressured ways:

  • Pray for wisdom, humility, clarity, and protection for the work.
  • Review course content for biblical faithfulness, cultural respect, and practical usefulness.
  • Introduce pastors, missionaries, sponsors, educators, or ministry leaders who could offer feedback.
  • Provide technical help with MkDocs, GitHub Pages, accessibility, editing, or workflow improvements.
  • Test courses and suggest clearer questions, assignments, or facilitator notes.
  • Help identify legal, safety, child protection, privacy, or compliance issues that need qualified review.
  • Provide financial support if appropriate and if a responsible receiving process is established.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: This is an early-stage but serious effort. The public site, policy framework, course template, and first course structure are being built now. The project should be evaluated as an emerging educational resource, not as a finished training ecosystem or accredited program.

docs.title: Course Catalog
docs.text:
The course catalog is a developing training library for missionary readiness. It is designed for independent missionaries, church members, mission-minded families, local churches, and sponsoring partners who need practical preparation before or alongside field-specific training.

The courses are introductory. They do not replace local church discernment, organizational requirements, professional advice, or supervised field preparation.

Courses are built to support careful study, guided reflection, practical assignments, and better readiness conversations.

docs.title: Level 1 Starter Courses
docs.text:
The following Level 1 courses are designed for new field workers, church members, and sponsoring partners who need practical, action-oriented readiness habits with low jargon and clear field application. Each course includes five lessons, an ungraded skill check in every lesson, and a graded quiz delivered through Google Forms.

Missionary Readiness 101 Level 145-60 minAvailable draft

The basic readiness framework for new learners, churches, sponsors, and families.

Spiritual Formation Level 160-75 minAvailable draft

Character, prayer, Scripture, worship, rest, obedience, and accountability.

Cross-Cultural Service Level 160-75 minAvailable draft

Humility, listening, cultural learning, local leadership, and respectful service.

Missionary Care Level 160-75 minAvailable draft

Accountability, support, family health, boundaries, care plans, and sustainability.

Language Learning Level 145-60 minAvailable draft

Realistic expectations, listening-first practice, local helpers, and perseverance.

Field Readiness Level 160-75 minAvailable draft

Practical field entry, logistics, communication, risk awareness, and first-year priorities.

docs.title: Level 2 Field Practitioner Courses
docs.text:
The following courses are designed for experienced field practitioners, mentors, and sending partners who need deeper cultural mapping, discipleship, and movement methods.

Culture & Worldview Fieldbook Level 260-75 minAvailable draft

Field-ready guidance for mapping rituals, merit systems, and animism in sensitive cross-cultural settings.

Worldview Transformation & Avoiding Syncretism Level 260-75 minAvailable draft

Practical guidance for identifying worldview buckets and keeping Christian practices gospel-centered.

Biblical Missiology & Multiplication Level 275-90 minAvailable draft

Movement-minded methods for obedience-based discipleship, local ownership, and oral Bible study.

docs.title: Level 3 Leadership & Security Courses
docs.text:
The following course is designed for field directors, organizational leaders, and sending boards managing risk, evacuation, and continuity.

Advanced Field Security & Crisis Evacuation Level 375-90 minAvailable draft

Practical security planning, tiered evacuation, and corporate continuity for high-risk field operations.

Nonprofit Governance & Field Accounting Level 375-90 minAvailable draft

Governance and financial accountability guidance for small ministries and independent founders.

docs.title: Course Pattern
docs.text:
Each course uses a consistent lightweight structure:

  • Overview and purpose.
  • Learning objectives.
  • Short lessons or readings.
  • Reflection questions.
  • Knowledge checks.
  • Practical assignments.
  • Facilitator notes and completion criteria.

Each lesson includes an ungraded skill check to reinforce the main idea. Graded course quizzes are delivered through Google Forms.

docs.title: Recommended Sequence
docs.text:
1. Missionary Readiness 101
  2. Spiritual Formation
  3. Cross-Cultural Service
  4. Missionary Care
  5. Language Learning
  6. Field Readiness
  7. Culture & Worldview Fieldbook
  8. Worldview Transformation & Avoiding Syncretism
  9. Biblical Missiology & Multiplication
  10. Advanced Field Security & Crisis Evacuation

This sequence starts with foundational readiness, then moves into field practice, worldview mapping, movement methods, and organizational leadership.

No accreditation claim

These courses are not academic degrees or accredited certifications. Any future credential language should use "certificate of completion" unless formal accreditation is actually obtained.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
# Advanced Field Security & Crisis Evacuation

Advanced Field Security & Crisis Evacuation is an advanced course for field directors, organizational leaders, and sending boards. It helps leaders and teams develop more faithful, sustainable field methods.

!!! note "Advanced course"
    This course is intended for experienced practitioners, organizational leaders, and trusted sending partners.

## Course Purpose

This course gives leaders a practical, tiered risk management framework for keeping people and organizational resources safe while maintaining healthy ministry relationships.

## Intended Audience

- Field directors, organizational leaders, and sending boards.
  • Security coordinators in high-risk contexts.

  • Teams responsible for continuity, evacuation planning, and secure communications.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Field Readiness and at least one active cross-cultural assignment or team leadership role.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Explain the difference between institution-led mission and movement-led multiplication.
  • Describe a sustainable, non-extractive approach to local training and support.
  • Apply a tiered evacuation protocol in a field context.
  • Use story-based and oral methods to engage contextual learners.
  • Draft a practical plan for local disciple-making, security, or crisis readiness.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A draft security matrix and evacuation checklist tailored to a field team or sending organization.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Integrated vs. Defensive Security
  2. Tiered Risk Management and Situational Awareness
  3. The 5-Tier Evacuation Protocol
  4. Corporate Evacuation and Continuity Planning
  5. Secure Communications and Digital Hygiene
  6. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Suggested Reading & Resources
docs.text:
• Serving with Eyes Wide Open — cultural intelligence and risk awareness.
  • 100 Deadly Skills — practical safety habits for uncertain field environments.
  • Insafety — building situational awareness, physical preparation, and travel security.

  • Survival & Austere Medicine — emergency care for low-resource and austere environments.

    • Review this course overview.
    • Complete all lessons.
    • Discuss the practical output with a mentor, field leader, or sending team.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. What does success look like for this course in your context?
  2. Where do you see the greatest risk of creating dependency instead of local ownership?
  3. Who must be involved in this plan for it to be sustainable?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Draft a one-page plan for your team, church, or local partner that applies the course principles in a specific field context.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the course overview and all lessons.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Share your draft plan with a supervisor, mentor, or sending partner.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Encourage learners to adapt these principles to the local church, not to impose a foreign model.
  • Emphasize local verification, cultural humility, and stewardship.
  • Remind teams that safety planning and multiplication planning belong together.

# Biblical Missiology & Multiplication

Biblical Missiology & Multiplication is an advanced course for field practitioners leading local disciple-making efforts. It helps leaders and teams develop more faithful, sustainable field methods.

!!! note "Advanced course"
    This course is intended for experienced practitioners, organizational leaders, and trusted sending partners.

## Course Purpose

This course helps leaders shift from traditional institution-focused mission models to movement-minded, obedience-based discipleship and local ownership.

## Intended Audience

- Field practitioners leading local disciple-making efforts.
  • Team leaders, mentors, and sending churches.

  • Local trainers using obedience-based models.

docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101, Cross-Cultural Service, and one or more practical field mentoring relationships.

  • Explain the difference between institution-led mission and movement-led multiplication.
  • Describe a sustainable, non-extractive approach to local training and support.
  • Apply a tiered evacuation protocol in a field context.
  • Use story-based and oral methods to engage contextual learners.
  • Draft a practical plan for local disciple-making, security, or crisis readiness.

docs.text: Using story, narrative, and oral methods to reach oral learners and reshape worldviews.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Learn how this principle supports faithful fieldwork, local ownership, and long-term effectiveness.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• Using story, narrative, and oral methods to reach oral learners and reshape worldviews.
  • Why a shallow or foreign method can undermine local multiplication.
  • How to choose one practical habit to apply immediately.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Identify one current approach in your context that should be changed to support sustainable local work.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Use this lesson to shape a more sustainable, obedient, and locally led approach.

docs.title: Community & Worldview Fieldbook
docs.text:
Community & Worldview Fieldbook is a Level 2 Field Practitioner course for active workers and small teams who need practical tools for mapping local meaning, avoiding syncretism, and discipling within community context.

Level 2 Field Practitioner

This course is designed for field practitioners, local partners, and small teams. It includes practical worksheets and should be reviewed by experienced leaders before use.

docs.title: Course purpose
docs.text: This course teaches field workers how to identify local worldview assumptions, distinguish cultural conformity from true discipleship, and use community insights responsibly in gospel communication.

docs.title: Intended audience
docs.text:
• Active field workers in sensitive cross-cultural settings
  • Small teams learning to engage local communities faithfully
  • Mentors and sending partners supporting field practitioners

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101, Culture & Worldview Fieldbook, or Cross-Cultural Service.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Identify when local people are being pressured to conform to foreign cultural norms instead of following Christ
  • Sort local practices into required, prohibited, and neutral cultural buckets
  • Evaluate teaching stories for local clarity and Gospel relevance
  • Map local spiritual authorities and the community’s core priorities
  • Harvest proverbs and idioms as windows into cultural logic and consequence

docs.title: Estimated time
docs.text: 90-110 minutes.

docs.title: Practical output
docs.text: A fieldbook starter set of notes, worksheet entries, and story filters for one local community.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Conformity vs. True Discipleship (The Local Snakes)
  2. The Three Buckets of Legalism
  3. The Illustration Filter (Communicating in Context)
  4. Mapping the Players and Core Foci
  5. Harvesting Proverbs and Idioms

docs.title: Field tools
docs.text:
• The Three Buckets Worksheet
  • Illustration Filter Matrix
  • Spiritual Players Profile
  • Local Proverb & Idiom Tracker

docs.title: Required reading/viewing
docs.text:
• Read this course overview.
  • Complete all five lessons.
  • Use the field tools with at least one local story, proverb, or practice.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. When has a new believer been asked to adopt foreign religious behavior instead of following Christ?
  2. Which local authority figures shape community decisions in the area you are studying?
  3. What proverb or idiom has already taught you something important about local priorities?

docs.title: Practical assignment
docs.text: Choose one local story, proverb, or spiritual authority figure and work through the course tools to map meaning, risk, and gospel relevance.

docs.title: Completion criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and all five lessons.
  • Use at least one field tool in a real or observed community situation.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Discuss your findings with a mentor or local partner.

docs.title: Facilitator notes
docs.text:
• Encourage learners to validate observations with local insiders.
  • Emphasize that cultural mapping is a practice, not a one-time judgement.
  • Avoid turning local stories into simple Western categories without consulting community knowledge.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A practical tool to evaluate whether a teaching story or illustration is appropriate for the local community.

docs.title: How to use this matrix
docs.text:
1. List the story or illustration you plan to use.
  2. Identify whether it fits Category 1 through Category 5.
  3. Choose the clearest and most locally relevant story available.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
• Category 1: Foreign concepts nobody understands
  • Category 2: Foreign concepts some may understand
  • Category 3: Familiar concepts with foreign values
  • Category 4: Locally experienced and understood
  • Category 5: Experienced but not understood; useful as a bridge

docs.title: Matrix
docs.text: Story or illustration Category Why it fits here Local clarity Gospel bridge

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text:
• Prefer Category 4 whenever possible.
  • Use Category 5 only when it connects with shared experience and points to Gospel truth.
  • Avoid Category 1 illustrations that depend on foreign institutions or assumptions.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Test one local illustration with a trusted community partner before using it in teaching or preaching.

docs.title: Lesson 1: Conformity vs. True Discipleship (The Local Snakes)
Course/series
docs.text: Community & Worldview Fieldbook

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field practitioners working in communities with strong local spiritual systems
  • Small teams wanting to disciple without imposing foreign cultural norms
  • Mentors guiding workers through community-sensitive ministry

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Help learners recognize when local people are being asked to conform to foreign cultural standards instead of following Christ, and to identify the real spiritual dangers present in the community.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Identify three signs of cultural conformity disguised as discipleship
  • Compare foreign religious expectations with local spiritual threats
  • Choose a gospel response that honors local identity and spiritual reality

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: True discipleship honors local identity while calling people to Christ, not to foreign cultural habits.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Missionaries often tell new believers to abandon local practices and adopt western forms of faith, which can leave them vulnerable to “local snakes” they still do not understand.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Conformity vs. contextual discipleship
  • Imported spiritual forms may hide local danger
  • The local snake metaphor for unseen community threats

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a three-column grid: (1) foreign conformity expectations, (2) local spiritual dangers, (3) gospel response that preserves identity.

docs.title: 5-Step Cultural Intelligence Process
docs.text: Step Description Field action Observation What is the specific behavior or ritual I am seeing? Record the behavior without judgment. Interpretation What worldview assumption or core focus is driving this behavior? Note the likely cultural meaning. Verification How can I confirm this interpretation with a trusted local cultural insider? Ask a local partner or interpreter. Reflection How does a biblical worldview challenge or fulfill the need this behavior is trying to address? Compare the cultural motive with Scripture. Ministry Implication How should our ministry adapt to present the gospel effectively in light of this? Adjust teaching, relationships, or practices.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A worker asks new believers to stop wearing local protective items and attend a foreign-style prayer group, while ignoring a nearby shrine’s influence on the community’s decisions.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• List the local behaviors being labeled as "required discipleship."
  • Identify what local spiritual threats remain if those behaviors change.
  • Note whether the response is protective, punitive, or kingdom-shaped.

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What local practice is being pushed as a sign of faith in your context?
  2. How does the community describe its own spiritual dangers?
  3. What is a gospel-shaped alternative that does not erase local identity?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Observe a conversation or teaching moment in the community. Map the foreign expectations, the local spiritual risks, and a contextual gospel alternative.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• The Culture Map by Erin Meyer
  • Ministering Cross-culturally by Lingenfelter & Mayers
  • Cross-Cultural Connections by Duane H. Elmer
  • When Helping Hurts by Corbett & Fikkert
  • Global Dexterity by Andy Molinsky
  • Is That Really You, God? by Loren Cunningham
  • Hudson Taylor’s Spiritual Secret
  • Missionary Readiness 101
  • Reflections from local partners on cultural conformity and spiritual authority

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Review this lesson with local partners to ensure the “local snakes” metaphor is culturally sensitive and accurate.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes

docs.title: Lesson 2: The Three Buckets of Legalism
Course/series
docs.text: Community & Worldview Fieldbook

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers leading discipleship in plural communities
  • Teams sorting cultural habits with biblical clarity
  • Leaders helping others resist rules-based religion

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners to sort local cultural behaviors and beliefs into the Top Bucket, Bottom Bucket, and Middle Bucket, then disciple locals to evaluate their own buckets biblically.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Define the Top, Bottom, and Middle Buckets for a community
  • Identify local practices that belong in each bucket
  • Help local believers apply biblical principles to their own culture

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Discipleship is about helping people sort their own cultural buckets, not emptying the middle bucket into a foreign classroom of grace.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers often treat the Middle Bucket as a place to remove all cultural options, which produces a rules-based religion instead of faithful contextual discipleship.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Top Bucket: required for community identity or survival
  • Bottom Bucket: prohibited behaviors or harmful practices
  • Middle Bucket: neutral, optional, or locally adapted practices

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a bucket worksheet to list local practices under three headings and ask: Does this belong to required life, prohibited harm, or optional local custom?

docs.title: The Three Buckets of Legalism
docs.text:
Bucket Description Example Top Bucket (Required) Behaviors a culture demands for acceptance, honor, or survival. Rituals or customs that determine social standing. Middle Bucket (Neutral/Optional) Practices that are culturally preferred but not morally required. This is the classroom of grace. Food choices, clothing styles, personal etiquette. Bottom Bucket (Prohibited) Taboos or harmful behaviors that result in shame or exclusion. Actions that the community marks as sinful or dangerous.

::: admonition danger If missionaries empty the Middle Bucket by forcing their own cultural rules into the Top or Bottom buckets, they create a foreign rules-based religion instead of faithful contextual discipleship. :::

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A team tells a local believer that middle-zone practices are wrong by default, even though they are neither sinful nor mandatory in that community.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Identify one practice for each bucket
  • Record the local meaning of each practice
  • Ask whether a biblical principle affirms, rejects, or leaves the practice optional

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. Which local custom did the team assign to the wrong bucket?
  2. When has a neutral behavior been treated like a moral requirement?
  3. How can you help locals decide their own bucket assignments?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Complete the Three Buckets Worksheet for one local ritual, custom, or belief. Share your bucket placement with a local partner or mentor.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• The Culture Map by Erin Meyer
  • Ministering Cross-culturally by Lingenfelter & Mayers
  • Cross-Cultural Connections by Duane H. Elmer
  • When Helping Hurts by Corbett & Fikkert
  • Global Dexterity by Andy Molinsky
  • Is That Really You, God? by Loren Cunningham
  • Hudson Taylor’s Spiritual Secret
  • The Three Buckets Worksheet
  • Insights from local partners on neutral cultural space

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Confirm the bucket categories with local practitioners before drawing conclusions about cultural compliance.

docs.title: Lesson 3: The Illustration Filter (Communicating in Context)
Course/series
docs.text: Community & Worldview Fieldbook

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers preparing stories, sermons, and teaching illustrations
  • Teams wanting to communicate clearly across cultural boundaries
  • Leaders who need a practical filter for Gospel illustration

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Evaluate teaching illustrations through five categories and prioritize stories that local people understand and can connect to the Gospel.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Describe the five categories of illustration relevance
  • Distinguish Category 1 from Category 4 illustrations
  • Use Category 5 as a bridge for Gospel communication

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Effective Gospel communication uses stories that the local community already understands, not foreign concepts they cannot receive.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Teachers often use illustrations that make sense only in a foreign culture, leaving locals confused or disconnected from the Gospel message.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Category 1: foreign concepts nobody understands
  • Category 4: locally experienced and understood stories
  • Category 5: experienced but not understood, useful as a Gospel bridge

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the Illustration Filter Matrix to place each story in a category and choose the highest category that still aligns with the Gospel.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A preacher uses a Western parable about bankers and contracts in a community where those institutions are unfamiliar, creating a Category 1 illustration.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• List the story’s images and assumptions
  • Assess whether the community experiences them firsthand
  • If not, ask whether a Category 4 or 5 story exists instead

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What story categories are common in your current teaching?
  2. Which local experience could become a better Gospel illustration?
  3. How can Category 5 help explain spiritual mystery without introducing foreign theology?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Build an illustration filter for one teaching point. Choose a Category 4 example if available, or a Category 5 bridge that leads naturally to the Gospel.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• The Culture Map by Erin Meyer
  • Ministering Cross-culturally by Lingenfelter & Mayers
  • Cross-Cultural Connections by Duane H. Elmer
  • When Helping Hurts by Corbett & Fikkert
  • Global Dexterity by Andy Molinsky
  • Is That Really You, God? by Loren Cunningham
  • Hudson Taylor’s Spiritual Secret
  • Illustration Filter Matrix
  • Case notes from local storytelling practice

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Review chosen illustrations with local partners to avoid unintentionally foreign or confusing imagery.

docs.title: Lesson 4: Mapping the Players and Core Foci
Course/series
docs.text: Community & Worldview Fieldbook

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers needing to identify local spiritual authorities
  • Teams mapping cultural priorities and influence patterns
  • Supervisors planning responsible ministry engagement

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Help learners map local spiritual players and contrast the culture’s core foci with the biblical worldview.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Identify key local spiritual authorities and their roles
  • Describe the community’s core foci and how they shape behavior
  • Compare those core foci with biblical priorities

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Understanding local authority figures and core priorities is essential for gospel witness that is both respectful and spiritually aware.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers may miss how local leaders and core cultural goals shape what people believe, making Gospel communication blind to the community’s actual concerns.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Spiritual players: monks, healers, amulets, elders
  • Core foci: surviving, avoiding bad karma, earning merit
  • Biblical priorities: grace, repentance, obedience, love

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the Spiritual Players Profile and a core foci chart to map authority, influence, and local priorities alongside Gospel truth.

docs.title: The Four-System Worldview Model
docs.text: System Core value What it drives Field implication Economy System (Wealth) Material wealth, survival, sustenance Food, clothing, shelter, cash production Address economic needs and how they shape spiritual choices. Karma/Merit System (Merit) Earning blessing, good luck, future standing Spiritual merit, ritual performance, reciprocity Show how grace differs from merit-based religion. Kinship System (Support Network) Social harmony and community safety Family honor, group belonging, relational support Respect communal ties while explaining Gospel community. Power System (Control) Controlling fate and appeasing spiritual forces Authority, rituals, protective practices Engage spiritual players without reinforcing fear-based control.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A community gives clear power to a traditional healer, yet workers continue teaching only through foreign church authority, missing the healer’s role.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• List the top local spiritual players
  • Record their influence, claims, and community role
  • Note the culture’s driving priorities and how they compare to biblical values

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. Who holds spiritual influence in this community?
  2. What core priority does the community seek most strongly?
  3. How can the Gospel address local priorities without replacing local identity unnecessarily?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Complete a Spiritual Players Profile for one community authority figure and chart the local core foci you observed.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• The Culture Map by Erin Meyer
  • Ministering Cross-culturally by Lingenfelter & Mayers
  • Cross-Cultural Connections by Duane H. Elmer
  • When Helping Hurts by Corbett & Fikkert
  • Global Dexterity by Andy Molinsky
  • Is That Really You, God? by Loren Cunningham
  • Hudson Taylor’s Spiritual Secret
  • Spiritual Players Profile
  • Local conversations about authority and values

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Validate the mapped players with trusted local sources to avoid misrepresenting authority structures.

docs.title: Course/series
docs.text: Community & Worldview Fieldbook

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers learning to read local values through language
  • Teams collecting cultural logic for Gospel communication
  • Mentors helping workers avoid misusing proverbs and idioms

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Show learners how to harvest local proverbs and idioms as windows into core cultural values, logic, and consequences.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Capture local proverbs and idioms accurately
  • Identify the values and worldviews embedded in them
  • Use proverbs as entry points for Gospel teaching

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Local proverbs and idioms reveal the culture’s logic in ways that foreign teaching methods often miss.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers may ignore proverbs or use them poorly, failing to see how they reveal the community’s moral reasoning and spiritual assumptions.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Proverbs as cultural snapshots
  • Idioms as embedded values and consequences
  • Proverbs as Gospel entry points

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the Local Proverb & Idiom Tracker to record the phrase, literal meaning, cultural logic, and Gospel application.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A team hears a proverb about "water and mud" meaning purity through distance, then misses its implication for grace and forgiveness.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Record the exact proverb or idiom
  • Note who used it and in what situation
  • Write the local logic, desired outcome, and Gospel bridge

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What value does this proverb promote?
  2. How does it shape people’s understanding of consequences?
  3. What Gospel truth can be connected to this cultural image?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Collect three proverbs or idioms from local conversation. Fill in the tracker for at least one of them.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• The Culture Map by Erin Meyer
  • Ministering Cross-culturally by Lingenfelter & Mayers

  • Notes from trusted local language informants

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Share collected proverbs with local partners to confirm meaning and to avoid misusing sacred or private expressions.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Document local proverbs and idioms to uncover cultural logic, values, and windows for Gospel conversation.

docs.title: How to use this tracker
docs.text:
1. Record the exact proverb or idiom.
  2. Ask a local source for its literal meaning and common usage.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Capture and map local spiritual authorities, traditional healers, and faith influencers so workers can understand who shapes community belief.

docs.title: How to use this profile
docs.text:
1. Identify one spiritual player in the local community.
  2. Record their role, influence, and claims.
  3. Note how their authority interacts with local priorities.

docs.title: Profile template
docs.text:
• Name or title:
  • Community role:
  • Spiritual claims:
  • Level of influence:
  • Primary audience:
  • Practices or rituals:

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Include both formal and informal authority figures.

docs.text: Compare this profile with the community’s core priorities and plan a Gospel response that respects local authority structures.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Provide a simple field tool for sorting local cultural practices and beliefs into the Top Bucket, Bottom Bucket, and Middle Bucket.

docs.title: How to use this worksheet
docs.text:
1. Identify one local practice, belief, or custom.
  2. Ask whether it is required, prohibited, or optional in the local context.
  3. Use the worksheet columns to record the meaning and your gospel response.

docs.title: Worksheet
docs.text: Bucket Local example Local meaning Biblical question Gospel response Top Bucket Bottom Bucket Middle Bucket

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Top Bucket items are seen as required for identity, survival, or belonging.
  • Bottom Bucket items are viewed as harmful or prohibited.
  • Middle Bucket items are optional, neutral, or culturally different without moral weight.

docs.title: Suggested next step

docs.title: Crisis Management & Organizational Member Care
docs.text:
Crisis Management & Organizational Member Care is a Level 3 Team/Leader course for leaders and organizations responsible for severe incidents, high-risk member care, and emergency systems.

Leadership course

This course is designed for organizational leaders, member care coordinators, and security or support teams who need structured crisis systems.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course teaches leaders how to build robust member care structures, define decision-making authority, activate crisis teams, and manage evacuation or trauma response.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Team leaders, field directors, and security coordinators
  • Member care staff and pastoral care teams
  • Sending organizations, sending churches, and support agencies

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Advanced Field Security & Crisis Evacuation and Missionary Care.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Apply the 7 S's framework to member care systems.
  • Define decision-making authority before emergencies occur.
  • Activate and staff a Crisis Management Team with clear communications.
  • Distinguish hibernation, relocation, and evacuation responses.
  • Follow trauma triage and care protocols after a field death.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 120-140 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A 7 S's member care audit, a CMT charter, and an evacuation trigger matrix.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. The 7 S's of Member Care
  2. Pre-Crisis Planning and Decision-Making Authority (DMA)
  3. The Crisis Management Team (CMT) & Communications
  4. Incident Response: Hibernation, Relocation, and Evacuation
  5. Trauma Triage and Death of a Field Worker

docs.title: Field tools
docs.text:
• The 7 S's Member Care Audit
  • Crisis Management Team (CMT) Charter
  • Field Emergency Evacuation Matrix

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Which of the 7 S's is weakest in your organization?
  2. Who currently holds DMA for severe field incidents?
  3. How would your team move if the security threshold changed overnight?

docs.title: Practical assignment
docs.text: Complete the 7 S's audit, draft a CMT charter, and define evacuation triggers for one active field location.

docs.title: Completion criteria
docs.text:
• Review all lessons and the three templates.
  • Identify a DMA leader and a primary crisis communication channel.
  • Share the audit results with organizational leadership.

docs.title: Facilitator notes
docs.text:
• Encourage teams to discuss real cases without naming sensitive individuals.
  • Emphasize that preparation and authority are more important than control.
  • Connect trauma response protocols to experienced clinical or pastoral specialists.

docs.title: Lesson 01: The 7 S's of Member Care
Lesson overview
docs.text: The 7 S's framework helps leaders identify the systems needed to develop, preserve, and restore workers in high-risk ministry.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Member care is most effective when it covers Savior care, Self-care, Staff care, Shepherd care, Structure care, Sender/Supporter care, and Specialist care.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Without intentional systems, workers can be left isolated, exhausted, or emotionally injured during crisis and routine field stress.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This framework applies across sending structures, cross-cultural teams, and field ministries where workers face spiritual, emotional, and physical risks.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should evaluate each of the 7 S's and build clear systems, not rely on informal goodwill or chance.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should support one another, know where to find resources, and stop harmful assumptions that care is optional.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Gather available policies, team care practices, and specialist contacts.
  • Map current care systems against each of the 7 S's.
  • Identify gaps and priority actions.

docs.title: The 7 S's explained
docs.text:
1. Savior care: discipleship, spiritual formation, and mission clarity.
  2. Self-care: personal health, rest, and boundaries.
  3. Staff care: peer care, mutual support, and team rhythms.
  4. Shepherd care: pastoral oversight, counseling, and spiritual guidance.
  5. Structure care: logistics, policies, and safe systems.
  6. Sender/Supporter care: sending church, family, and donor relationships.
  7. Specialist care: clinical, medical, and trauma professionals.

docs.title: Step 1: Audit current care systems
docs.text: Review what each of the 7 S's looks like in your organization. Note who is responsible and how support is delivered.

docs.title: Step 2: Identify gaps
docs.text: Mark the weakest systems, such as absent specialist care or unclear pastoral support.

docs.title: Step 3: Plan improvements
docs.text: Prioritize actions that strengthen the weakest S's first, especially those that protect workers in crisis.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Many organizations focus on Sender care or Structure care and neglect Specialist care or staff mutual support.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A team implements a weekly peer check-in for Staff care while also clarifying emergency referral pathways for Specialist care.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Which of the 7 S's is the most developed in your organization?
  2. Which area needs an immediate improvement?
  3. Who will champion the weakest S's?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The 7 S's framework brings balance and accountability to member care. It is a practical way to evaluate support systems before crisis.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Use the 7 S's Member Care Audit tool to map your organization’s systems.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 02: Pre-Crisis Planning and Decision-Making Authority (DMA)
Lesson overview
docs.text: Clear planning and pre-defined authority are the strongest predictors of effective crisis response.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: The quality of crisis outcomes depends on decisions made before the incident occurs. Organizations must define Decision-Making Authority early.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Without DMA, organizations create confusion, delay action, and risk unauthorized responses that escalate harm.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: DMA matters in kidnapping, political collapse, medical emergencies, and any scenario where quick, coordinated action is required.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders must name who has authority, under what conditions, and how that authority communicates with the field.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Field teams should know who holds DMA, who to notify first, and how to execute instructions without second-guessing.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Draft a clear DMA policy.
  • Create an emergency contact list.
  • Establish tertiary communication channels.

docs.title: Defining DMA
docs.text: DMA should be explicit, documented, and understood by the field team, sending team, and support network.

docs.title: Step 1: Decide roles and authorities
docs.text: Identify the Crisis Manager, CMT leader, legal lead, security lead, and family care lead.

docs.title: Step 2: Document the process
docs.text: Record who decides, how decisions are escalated, and how approvals are communicated.

docs.title: Step 3: Share the plan
docs.text: Distribute the DMA policy to all stakeholders and review it regularly.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Many organizations wait until an incident occurs before naming authority. That delay can cost time and safety.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A ministry creates a DMA decision tree that identifies who manages communications, who approves evacuation, and who liaises with family.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Who currently holds DMA in your organization?
  2. What gaps exist in your emergency contact plan?
  3. How will you test your DMA assumptions?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Pre-crisis planning and clear DMA prevent paralysis. Define authority, document it, and ensure the field understands it.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Write or update your organization’s DMA policy and share it with the crisis leadership team.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 03: The Crisis Management Team (CMT) & Communications
Lesson overview
docs.text: The Crisis Management Team (CMT) is the nerve center for a severe incident. It protects the field from panic and manages official information.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: A CMT insulates crisis work from routine operations, keeps the field focused, and controls communication flow.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: When field workers are left without a crisis team, they can make rushed or unsafe decisions under stress.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: The CMT is activated in major incidents such as kidnapping, violent unrest, political collapse, or sudden mass displacement.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should appoint a CMT, define roles clearly, and establish communication protocols before activation.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Team members must trust the CMT, follow instructions, and avoid sharing unauthorized information.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Define the CMT membership and roles.
  • Create a communication flow chart.
  • Identify spokespeople and information gates.

docs.title: CMT roles explained
docs.text: The CMT typically includes a crisis manager, communications lead, legal advisor, member care lead, and logistics coordinator.

docs.title: Step 1: Activate the CMT
docs.text: When the trigger threshold is crossed, the Crisis Manager activates the team and announces the crisis protocol.

docs.title: Step 2: Manage communications
docs.text: The CMT controls official news releases, internal updates, and contact with family/supporters.

docs.title: Step 3: Advise and support the field
docs.text: The CMT provides guidance to the field without exposing them to unnecessary pressure or conflicting instructions.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Field teams may receive multiple messages from different sources. The CMT must make one official line and stop rumor spread.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: In a high-risk incident, the CMT authorizes one spokesperson, sets a holding statement, and informs the field of the next step.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Who should be on your CMT?
  2. How would your CMT handle press, family, and internal communications?
  3. What would stop the field from receiving conflicting directives?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: A strong CMT keeps crises contained and the field supported. Communication discipline is a core part of that strength.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Draft a simple CMT charter that defines roles, authority, and communication flow.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 04: Incident Response: Hibernation, Relocation, and Evacuation
Lesson overview
docs.text: Teams must distinguish between lockdown, internal relocation, and full evacuation. Predefined triggers decide when to move.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Knowing the difference between hibernation, relocation, and evacuation prevents reactive movement and protects lives.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Waiting too long to move can make evacuation impossible. Moving too soon wastes resources and damages credibility.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This matrix applies when security conditions change, authority collapses, or travel becomes dangerous.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should define thresholds and ensure the field understands the difference between each response.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Team members should know how to shelter in place, where to relocate internally, and when to prepare for evacuation.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Develop trigger matrices and decision points.
  • Plan safe locations for lockdown and relocation.
  • Confirm evacuation routes and support channels.

docs.title: Response definitions
docs.text:
• Hibernation: sheltering in place with limited movement.
  • Relocation: moving to another safe location within the country or region.
  • Evacuation: leaving the country or mission area.

docs.title: Step 1: Set trigger thresholds
docs.text: Define clear conditions for each response, such as violence, travel restrictions, or critical infrastructure failure.

docs.title: Step 2: Communicate the plan
docs.text: Share the trigger matrix with the field and practice how to move when thresholds are reached.

docs.title: Step 3: Execute the response
docs.text: Use the pre-approved decision tree and avoid ad hoc judgments when stress is high.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Teams often treat evacuation as the only option, when a safe period of lockdown or relocation may be better.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: When protests begin near the compound, the team moves to a secure internal site for hibernation rather than immediately evacuating.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What conditions would trigger lockdown in your context?
  2. When would you choose relocation over evacuation?
  3. How can you avoid waiting too long?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Clear trigger matrices allow teams to move on purpose, not out of panic. Distinguish the three responses and plan accordingly.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Create or review your own response matrix and mark the current status of each field location.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 05: Trauma Triage and Death of a Field Worker
Lesson overview
docs.text: This lesson covers the worst-case responses: trauma triage, local legal requirements, memorial planning, and grief care.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Handling a field death requires immediate leadership, local legal sensitivity, and long-term care for survivors.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: A poorly managed death can inflict additional trauma on the team, damage relationships with local authorities, and harm the family.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to medical death, violence, accident, or any circumstance where a worker dies in the field.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should coordinate legal steps, communicate with family, and ensure survivors receive specialist grief care.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: The team should care for one another, preserve evidence if needed, and support the family with dignity and honor.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Know local death, funeral, and transport regulations.
  • Identify grief counselors and trauma specialists.
  • Prepare a family communication plan.

docs.title: Trauma triage explained
docs.text: Triage after a field death means assessing who needs medical care, psychological support, and practical help immediately.

docs.title: Step 1: Stabilize survivors
docs.text: Provide medical attention, safe shelter, and emotional support for those present.

docs.title: Step 2: Contact authorities and the family
docs.text: Follow legal requirements and notify the family through a designated point person.

docs.title: Step 3: Provide specialist care
docs.text: Arrange grief counseling, pastoral care, and debriefing for team members and the family.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Teams may avoid discussing death until it happens. Planning in advance reduces confusion and harm.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A ministry has a checklist for local authorities, embalming/transport steps, and grief support, which the Crisis Manager uses immediately.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What would your team need first if a worker died in the field?
  2. Who would handle legal, family, and team care communication?
  3. Where would survivors get trauma counseling?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Death in the field requires careful coordination, compassion, and specialist support. Advance planning makes the response more faithful.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Review your organization’s current death-care protocols and identify one missing resource.

Back Course Overview

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A diagnostic tool for ministries to evaluate their support systems across the seven member care domains.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text: Rate each item from 1 (weak) to 5 (strong). Note gaps and assign follow-up actions.

docs.title: Audit table
docs.text: S Description Current rating (1-5) Strengths Gaps Next action Savior care Discipleship, spiritual formation, and mission clarity Self-care Personal health, rest, and boundaries Staff care Peer support, mutual encouragement, team rhythms Shepherd care Pastoral oversight, counseling, mentoring Structure care Policies, logistics, safety systems Sender/Supporter care Sending church, family, and supporter relationships Specialist care Access to clinical, medical, or trauma professionals

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use this audit annually and after any major incident.
  • Include field workers, sending leaders, and care specialists in the review.
  • Treat low ratings as opportunities for system-building rather than blame.

docs.title: Cross-Cultural Service
docs.text:
Cross-Cultural Service helps learners enter new communities as humble listeners who can learn culture, build trust, and serve without assuming they already understand.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course introduces cultural humility, local respect, and practical listening habits for missionary service.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective cross-cultural workers.
  • Church members and families preparing for service.
  • Sponsors and small organizations supporting workers.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Explain the difference between cultural awareness and cultural humility.
  • Practice observation and listening before offering solutions.
  • Identify assumptions that can harm cross-cultural relationships.
  • Prepare questions that support learning from local believers and community members.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A cultural learning fieldbook starter and a reproducible storying habit sheet.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Entering As A Learner
  2. Listening and Observation Practices
  3. Local Authority, Ritual, and Respect
  4. Narrative Learning and Storying
  5. Reproducible Group Habits
  6. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete all five lessons.
  • Review one reputable resource on cross-cultural listening or local leadership.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. What assumptions might you bring into a new culture?
  2. Who are the local leaders you would need to learn from?
  3. What would it look like to slow down before trying to help?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Interview someone with meaningful cross-cultural experience. Ask what helped them learn well and what mistakes they would caution against.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and all lessons.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Complete the interview assignment and summarize what you learned.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Keep learners from treating culture as a problem to solve.
  • Emphasize local churches, local leaders, and patient learning.
  • Address superiority, savior language, and careless assumptions directly but constructively.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Entering As A Learner
docs.text: Cross-cultural service begins with humility. A learner enters a new setting with respect, patience, and a willingness to be corrected.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Before serving well, learners must first learn to observe, listen, ask, and receive local wisdom.

docs.title: Listen Before Explaining
docs.text: New workers should resist quick conclusions. Listening helps reveal what people value, fear, celebrate, and need.

docs.title: Honor Local Leaders
docs.text: Local churches and leaders understand context in ways outsiders usually do not. Responsible workers seek their counsel and avoid bypassing them.

docs.title: Notice Assumptions
docs.text: Every learner carries assumptions about time, authority, family, money, communication, worship, and leadership. Naming those assumptions helps prevent harm.

docs.title: Serve With Patience
docs.text: Trust grows slowly. Faithful service often begins with small acts of reliability rather than visible leadership.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. What do you tend to assume too quickly?
  2. What questions would help you learn from local leaders?
  3. How can you practice patient listening now?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Cross-cultural service requires a learner's posture. Humility is not weakness; it is part of faithful preparation.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: The first gift a worker offers is attention. Listening before speaking builds trust and reduces harm.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Learn how focused listening and observation open the door to local understanding.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• The first gift a worker offers is attention. Listening before speaking builds trust and reduces harm.
  • How the idea connects to the course output.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Practice one observation method today and note what it taught you about local life.

docs.title: Lesson 03: Local Authority, Ritual, and Respect
Key Idea
docs.text: Rituals and local authority are not just cultural decorations; they explain how people understand identity, power, and belonging.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Recognize when local rituals and authority structures shape meaning.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• Rituals and local authority are not just cultural decorations; they explain how people understand identity, power, and belonging.
  • How the idea connects to the course output.

docs.text: Storying is a field-ready way to share truth in cultures that learn through narrative, proverbs, and drama.

docs.title: Culture & Worldview Fieldbook
docs.text:
Culture & Worldview Fieldbook is a Level 2 course for active workers and small teams operating in cross-cultural environments. It teaches field practitioners how to map local worldview assumptions and communicate the gospel without creating syncretism.

Field Practitioner course

This course is intended for active workers who need practical cultural mapping tools. It should be reviewed by experienced field leaders and local partners before use.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course teaches learners to identify the worldview assumptions that shape local rituals, merit systems, and animistic thinking so they can present the gospel clearly and faithfully.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Active field workers in cross-cultural settings.
  • Small teams working with sensitive communities.
  • Mentors and sending partners supporting field practitioners.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101 and Cross-Cultural Service.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Describe the six worldview lenses.
  • Explain why animism can lead to syncretism without careful teaching.
  • Use a structured observation model to interpret cultural practice.
  • Map one local ritual through a practical fieldbook framework.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A completed Culture & Worldview Fieldbook Starter Worksheet mapping one local ritual through observation, interpretation, verification, reflection, and ministry implication.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. The Six Worldview Lenses
  2. Understanding Animism and Avoiding Syncretism
  3. The Observation to Implication Framework
  4. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read this course overview.
  • Complete all three lessons.
  • Consider a local ritual or practice you will map with the fieldbook worksheet.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. Which worldview lens feels easiest to observe? Which feels hardest?
  2. How might a local ritual be mistaken for a Biblical practice by an untrained worker?
  3. What question will you ask a local insider before drawing a conclusion?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Download or build a Culture & Worldview Fieldbook Starter Worksheet. Choose a local ritual and map it through the Observation to Implication framework.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and all lessons.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft a fieldbook map for one local ritual and discuss it with a local cultural insider or mentor.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Emphasize that worldview mapping is a practice, not a one-time analysis.
  • Help learners avoid quick judgments and encourage local verification.
  • Review whether the fieldbook worksheet is faithful to the community's lived reality.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Field practitioners map culture by looking past surface customs and identifying the worldview assumptions that shape them.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Learn the six foundational lenses that reveal how a society understands reality.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches

  • Why field practitioners must observe more than customs.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Use these six lenses as the foundation for every worldview map.

docs.title: Lesson 02: Understanding Animism and Avoiding Syncretism
Key Idea
docs.text: A clear field practice must separate Christian grace from animistic merit and manipulation.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Understand how animistic assumptions can produce syncretism if believers are not taught a biblical worldview.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• Why syncretism is often a mix of Christian and incompatible worldview assumptions.
  • How animism treats spirits as manipulable forces.
  • Why the gospel must be explained as relationship with a wise, loving God rather than a magic system.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Write one short paragraph describing how you would explain to a local learner that God is not a spirit to be bribed, but a loving Creator who calls for relationship.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Teach the gospel as grace and relationship, not ritual recipes or spirit control.

docs.title: Lesson 03: The Observation to Implication Framework
Key Idea
docs.text: A consistent field model helps practitioners turn confusing rituals into reliable ministry insight.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Practice a structured five-step model for interpreting cultural practice and its ministry implications.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• Observation, Interpretation, Verification, Reflection, and Ministry Implication.
  • How to move from a specific ritual to a practical gospel response.
  • Why local verification is essential before drawing conclusions.

docs.title: Practical Action

docs.title: Ethical Fundraising & Partner Communication
docs.text:
Ethical Fundraising & Partner Communication equips field practitioners to build support-raising systems that honor donors, protect local dignity, and treat money as a means to mission, not an end.

Level 2 course

This course is designed for independent workers, support teams, and field communicators who need ethical financial practices in challenging contexts.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: To help learners overcome the shame of asking for support, invite partners into mission responsibly, and manage gifts with integrity.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Independent missionaries and project leaders.
  • Support-raising coordinators.
  • Church and team communication partners.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101 and Field Readiness.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Explain the biblical foundation for support raising.
  • Build a partnership team without reducing people to transactions.
  • Manage restricted and unrestricted gifts with clear policies.
  • Tell ethical stories that protect local neighbors.
  • Communicate safely in sensitive and restricted contexts.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 70-90 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A risk-screened communication checklist, a media consent form, and a gift acceptance policy.

1. The Biblical Foundation of Support Raising
  2. Developing a Partnership Team & The "God Ask"
  3. Managing Restricted Gifts vs. Unrestricted Gifts

  5. Sensitive-Context Communication & Digital Security

  2. What risks should you screen for in donor communications?
  3. Who should review your gift acceptance policy before it is used?

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A checklist to screen newsletters, prayer letters, and digital updates for security and dignity risks.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text: Review each item before sending a donor communication. Mark any risks and revise accordingly.

docs.title: Risk checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Does the communication protect the identity of local believers?
  • [ ] Does it avoid real photos linked to pseudonyms?
  • [ ] Does it avoid exact location details that could identify a field site?
  • [ ] Does it avoid names of minors, vulnerable adults, or at-risk churches?
  • [ ] Does it avoid language that exploits poverty or trauma for emotional impact?
  • [ ] Does it clearly separate prayer requests from financial appeals?
  • [ ] Does it honor donor intent and avoid misleading promises?
  • [ ] Does it include a respectful consent statement if using media?
  • [ ] Has a team member or supervisor reviewed the message for safety?
  • [ ] Is the update transparent without over-sharing sensitive information?

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use this checklist with every public update in restricted or sensitive contexts.
  • Adjust items for your legal and cultural environment.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A framework defining how restricted and unrestricted gifts are processed, approved, and documented.

docs.title: Policy statement
docs.text: Our ministry accepts gifts responsibly, honoring donor intent and protecting organizational integrity.

docs.title: Gift categories
docs.text:
• Unrestricted gifts: funds that may be used for general ministry purposes.
  • Restricted gifts: funds designated for a specific project, activity, or timeframe.
  • Conditional gifts: funds given with conditions that must be accepted or declined.

docs.title: Donor intent
docs.text: Donor intent must be captured in writing for every restricted or large gift. If intent is unclear, the ministry will seek clarification before acceptance.

docs.title: Approval process
docs.text:
• Gifts under $____ may be accepted by the field leader.
  • Restricted or large gifts require approval from: _________
  • The gift acceptance committee or board must review and document the decision.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Record donor name, amount, gift purpose, and date.
  • Attach donor instructions and any written clarifications.
  • Store records for at least ___ years according to local legal requirements.

docs.title: Use of funds
docs.text: Restricted gifts will be used only for the designated purpose unless the donor agrees to a change.

docs.title: Review
docs.text: This policy will be reviewed annually or whenever gift handling procedures change.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Review this policy with legal and financial advisors.
  • Do not accept a gift that conflicts with the ministry’s values or mission.

docs.title: Knowledge Check
docs.text:
This course uses a graded quiz delivered through Google Forms after finishing the lessons.

  • Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Format: multiple choice and short answer
  • Link: Complete the graded course quiz

  • Partnership mapping and the God Ask
  • Ethical storytelling and media consent
  • Donor retention and pastoral care

docs.title: Preparation

• Review the lesson skill checks.
  • Complete the practical output: donor calendar and consent policy.
