docs.title: Lesson 01: The Biblical Foundation of Support Raising
Lesson overview
docs.text: Financial shortage is the number one hindrance to field objectives. This lesson helps learners reframe fundraising as inviting partners into God’s mission rather than begging.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Support raising is a biblical partnership, not a personal shame or a transactional burden.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Workers who avoid asking for support often suffer in silence and leave field objectives unmet.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This lesson is especially relevant for workers who must raise support in restricted or relational settings and who cannot rely on steady income.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner must identify potential partners, craft a faithful ask, and own the responsibility to communicate clearly.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should affirm support raising as ministry and help normalize the practice within the community.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Review biblical examples of partnership-based giving.
  • Compare tentmaking, supported ministry, and shared partnership models.
  • Write a short missional support statement.

docs.title: Step 1: Reframe the need
docs.text: Recognize that asking for support is an invitation to join the mission, not a plea for charity.

docs.title: Step 2: Describe the model
docs.text: Explain the difference between tentmaker income and a supported worker model in simple, honest terms.

docs.title: Step 3: Remove shame
docs.text: Use biblical language, prayer, and relational transparency to remove shame from the ask.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Workers often feel guilty about asking. The antidote is to focus on the shared calling and the partner’s role, not on money alone.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A worker explains to a church, “We are asking you to partner with us in ministry by funding our living costs so we can stay focused on long-term work.”

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What is your honest fear about asking for support?
  2. How can you frame your funding need as mission partnership?
  3. Which scriptural example helps you speak with confidence?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The biblical foundation for support raising turns financial need into a shared mission opportunity.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Write a one-paragraph support invitation that describes your calling, the need, and the partner’s role.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not financial or legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney or financial professional for legal, tax, or compliance questions.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 02: Developing a Partnership Team & The "God Ask"
Lesson overview
docs.text: This lesson teaches practical partner mapping and the steps to make a faithful "God Ask" without turning supporters into transactions.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: A partnership team is a network of people, not a donor list; the ask should invite participation, not pressure.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: When supporters feel used, relationships break down. Ethical fundraising builds long-term trust.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to email networks, church teams, personal contacts, and community-based supporters.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner should map relationships honestly, track contact needs respectfully, and communicate gratitude regularly.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should help identify shared prayer supporters, financial partners, and communication allies.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• List existing supporters, prayer partners, and potential new contacts.
  • Group contacts by relationship type rather than gift potential.
  • Prepare brief praise reports and prayer updates.

docs.title: Step 1: Map the network
docs.text: Create a partner map with categories such as prayer supporters, financial partners, mentor partners, and communication partners.

docs.title: Step 2: Plan the God Ask
docs.text: Decide who to ask, why you are asking them, and what partnership looks like for each person.

docs.title: Step 3: Use relational follow-up
docs.text: Track conversations, send thank-you notes, and share progress honestly rather than only when asking for money.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: It is easy to treat people as transactions when you only track gifts. Focus first on relationship and shared calling.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A worker sends a short note to a small group of supporters, describing the need, the shared mission, and how they can pray and give.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Who is already on your partnership team?
  2. What is one way to follow up without asking for money?
  3. How will you balance gratitude and requests?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Developing a partnership team is a relational process. The God Ask should invite participation in mission, not create pressure.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Build a partner map and draft one God Ask for a close supporter group.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not financial or legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney or financial professional for legal, tax, or compliance questions.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 03: Managing Restricted Gifts vs. Unrestricted Gifts
Lesson overview
docs.text: Every gift must have a source, purpose, approval path, and documentation. This lesson helps learners manage restricted and unrestricted donations ethically.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Money is only ethical when the giver’s intent is clear and the organization’s response is documented.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Unclear gift handling damages trust, harms donors, and makes the ministry vulnerable to misuse.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to cash gifts, large unmarked donations, project-specific support, and in-kind contributions.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner should ask clarifying questions, record donor intent, and follow the organization’s gift acceptance policy.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should protect restricted gifts from being used for unintended purposes and track each gift’s approval path.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Review existing gift handling practices.
  • Identify who approves restricted gifts.
  • Draft clear documentation steps.

docs.title: Step 1: Classify gifts
docs.text: Determine whether each gift is unrestricted, project-restricted, time-restricted, or conditional.

docs.title: Step 2: Clarify donor intent
docs.text: Ask donors to state their purpose in writing and confirm any ambiguities before accepting large or unusual gifts.

docs.title: Step 3: Document approvals
docs.text: Record the approval process so restricted gifts are only used as intended.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Large unmarked gifts can create pressure to spend quickly. The right response is to pause, clarify, and document.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A donor offers a large gift without specifying a project. The team asks for written direction, records the gift, and only releases funds after approval.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What questions will you ask when a donor gives a restricted gift?
  2. How will you document donor intent?
  3. Who should approve the release of restricted funds?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Managing restricted and unrestricted gifts is foundational for ethical fundraising.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Use the gift acceptance policy template to define your approval and documentation process.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not financial or legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney or financial professional for legal, tax, or compliance questions.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 04: Ethical Storytelling and Avoiding Poverty Porn
Lesson overview
docs.text: A good story can endanger local believers, children, or projects if communication is not risk-screened.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Ethical storytelling protects the dignity, privacy, and safety of the people featured.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Careless stories can expose locals to harm, create false expectations, or exploit trauma for sympathy.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to social media, newsletters, video updates, and any communication that mentions people or places in sensitive settings.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner should prioritize consent, privacy, and the wellbeing of the people being shared.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should review stories together and ensure partners understand the local risks.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Review local safeguarding and media guidelines.
  • Identify sensitive topics, locations, and people.
  • Develop rules for image selection and naming.

docs.title: Step 1: Screen risks
docs.text: Ask whether the story could identify vulnerable people, reveal protected locations, or create stigma.

docs.title: Step 2: Protect dignity
docs.text: Choose descriptions that honor people rather than depicting them as needy or helpless.

docs.title: Step 3: Avoid poverty porn
docs.text: Do not use dramatic language or images that exploit suffering for fundraising impact.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: It is tempting to use bold language for attention. The ethical choice is to tell a true story without turning people into objects.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A newsletter shares a brief, respectful update about a local community without naming individuals or showing identifiable faces.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What risks should you screen for in your next story?
  2. How can you protect the privacy of local partners?
  3. What language will you avoid when describing need?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Ethical storytelling is a protective discipline. It keeps communication compelling without exploiting people.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Review the draft Photo/Media Consent Form and apply it to your next update.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not financial or legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney or privacy professional for questions about consent, media, or legal exposures.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson overview
docs.text: In restricted contexts, careless communication can jeopardize lives. This lesson defines the rules for safe digital updates, pseudonyms, and social media.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: When communication is sensitive, a pseudonym must never be paired with a real photo of the field worker or local contacts.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Loose information can expose workers, believers, and local partners to surveillance, arrest, or violence.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to restricted countries, anonymous support networks, and any situation where online content can be traced back to individuals.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner should follow strict security guidelines and treat every public update as potentially visible to hostile actors.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should agree on shared protocols, review messages before publication, and keep sensitive details off public platforms.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Identify which channels are safe for public updates.
  • Decide which names, locations, and images are off-limits.
  • Choose pseudonyms when needed and keep them separate from real identifying information.

docs.title: Step 1: Use pseudonyms carefully
docs.text: A pseudonym can protect identity, but it must not be paired with an actual photo of the worker or local people.

docs.title: Step 2: Screen every story
docs.text: Review newsletters, social media posts, and prayer requests for security and privacy risks.

docs.title: Step 3: Secure digital habits
docs.text: Use strong passwords, avoid public Wi-Fi for sensitive work, and keep devices updated.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Workers often assume small details are harmless. Safety requires treating every detail as potentially dangerous.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A team publishes a prayer letter with a pseudonym and generic location description, while keeping names, photos, and exact coordinates out of the update.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What information would you never share publicly from your current context?
  2. How will you separate a pseudonym from identifying photos and details?
  3. Who will review your next update for security concerns?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Sensitive communication requires intentional safety checks. Use pseudonyms, protect photos, and keep storytelling disciplined.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Apply the Donor Communication Risk Guide to your next newsletter or update.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not legal, financial, or security advice. Consult a qualified compliance, legal, or security professional for your context.

docs.title: Field Language Acquisition
docs.text:
Field Language Acquisition is a Level 2 Field Practitioner course based on the LACE methodology. It is designed for workers in contexts without formal language schools, where learners depend on local helpers, simple materials, and daily practice.

Field Practitioner course

This course is designed for active field learners, tentmakers, and team members who need language acquisition methods that work outside classrooms.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course equips learners to manage their own language learning, use local language helpers wisely, and make steady progress through TPR, picture books, and real-life language acquisition projects.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers and teams without access to formal language school programs
  • Independent missionaries who need a practical, learner-driven language strategy
  • Sending partners and support leaders who coach language helpers and learners

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Language Learning or Cross-Cultural Service.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Take ownership of their language study process.
  • Use power-tool phrases to keep sessions in the target language.
  • Build comprehension with TPR and culturally relevant picture books.
  • Design and complete language acquisition projects in the local setting.
  • Recognize the emotional challenges of immersive language learning.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 90-110 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A power-tool phrase list, a helper agreement, and a 90-day language acquisition plan.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. The Learner-Driven Model & "Power Tools"
  2. Total Physical Response (TPR) and Object Manipulation
  3. The Picture Book Technique
  4. Language Acquisition Projects (LAPs) & Shared Experiences
  5. The Emotional Toll of Language Learning

docs.title: Field tools
docs.text:
• The "Power Tools" Starter List
  • Language Helper Agreement
  • 90-Day Language Acquisition Plan (LAP)

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. How can you keep language learning in the target language when your helper wants to switch to English?
  2. What language project would be most useful in your current context?
  3. Who can help you monitor your emotional energy during language learning?

docs.title: Practical assignment
docs.text: Use the power tools, agree expectations with a helper, and draft a 90-day language acquisition plan for your next local interaction.

docs.title: Completion criteria
docs.text:
• Review the course overview and all five lessons.
  • Complete the power-tools checklist and helper agreement.
  • Draft a 90-day LAP and discuss it with a mentor or sending partner.

docs.title: Facilitator notes
docs.text:
• Encourage learners to treat language helpers as partners rather than teachers.
  • Watch for discouragement that comes from initial mistakes and slow progress.
  • Apply local cultural norms to helper agreements and project scenarios.

docs.text: A modular schedule that combines TPR, Picture Book review, and community language projects over 90 days.

docs.text: Fill in each weekly plan, choose one LAP project per month, and review progress with a mentor or helper.

docs.text:
• Month 1: Build listening, power tools, and basic commands.
  • Month 2: Deepen vocabulary with picture books and shared experiences.
  • Month 3: Execute language projects and strengthen emotional stamina.

docs.text:
• Month 1 LAP: A guided local conversation, market purchase, or greeting exchange.
  • Month 2 LAP: A shared activity with a community member, such as cooking or working together.
  • Month 3 LAP: A more complex task, such as leading a short prayer, teaching a phrase, or visiting a local service.

• Adjust the plan to your context and pace.

docs.text: This agreement describes how the helper will support the learner in developing language skills through regular sessions and careful correction.

docs.title: Helper responsibilities
docs.text:
• Meet with the learner as agreed, ideally 3–5 times per week.
  • Use the target language as much as possible.
  • Help the learner with pronunciation, repetition, and correction.
  • Explain simple vocabulary, gestures, and cultural norms.
  • Respect the learner’s dignity and emotional state.

docs.text:

  • Use power tool phrases to keep the session in the target language.
  • Review new words between sessions.
  • Be respectful of the helper’s time and service.
  • Share how the helper can improve the session.

• Use this agreement as a starting point and adapt it to local customs.

docs.title: Lesson overview
docs.text: Workers in the field are responsible for the pace, materials, and outcomes of their language learning. The learner-driven model makes the missionary, not a formal teacher, the central decision maker.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: The learner must own the process and use simple "power tool" phrases to keep the session in the target language.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Without learner control, helpers may drift into English or only repeat phrases without building comprehension. Language learning stalls when the learner feels passive.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This model is designed for environments without schools, classrooms, or formal curricula. It works with a helper, a notebook, and daily practice in the community.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner sets goals, chooses what to practice, asks for correction, and keeps the helper accountable for using the target language.

docs.title: Helper role
docs.text: The language helper provides input, models phrases, gives feedback, and listens while the learner uses power tools to direct the session.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Identify a trusted language helper.
  • Prepare a short list of immediate practical phrases.
  • Agree on the duration and frequency of sessions.

docs.title: Power tools explained
docs.text: Power tools are control phrases such as "Please repeat," "Speak slower," and "Correct my mistake." They let the learner manage the pace and keep the helper from translating into English.

docs.title: Step 1: Identify the first 20 phrases
docs.text: Choose 20 essential phrases that support instruction, correction, and practice. These should cover session control, feedback, and basic conversation glue.

docs.title: Step 2: Practice the tools first
docs.text: Before learning new vocabulary, learn the power tool phrases themselves. Use them until they become automatic in sessions.

docs.title: Step 3: Use the tools in every session
docs.text: Start each meeting by reviewing the power tools and then use them actively when you need the helper to slow down, repeat, or correct you.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Learners often feel embarrassed using correction phrases. They should remember that power tools protect their learning time and keep the session focused.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A learner says, "Please repeat," when the helper speaks too fast, and "Correct my mistake" when attempting a new sentence. This keeps the session in the target language.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Which power tool phrase feels hardest to use?
  2. How will you remind yourself to ask for repetition or correction?
  3. What will you do if the helper switches to English?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: A learner-driven model and power tools give the language learner authority in the session. This is the foundation for fast, field-based acquisition.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Use the power tools in your next meeting and compare the result with a session where you do not use them.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 02: Total Physical Response (TPR) and Object Manipulation
Lesson overview
docs.text: Total Physical Response (TPR) uses action and object interaction to help learners absorb meaning without translating mentally into English.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: TPR builds a subconscious feel for the language by pairing commands with physical movement and concrete objects.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Many learners try to translate every word. TPR bypasses that habit by teaching through doing first, which is more natural for beginners.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: TPR works well in homes, markets, churches, and locations where simple commands and objects are available. It requires no textbooks.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner listens carefully, follows commands, and uses the target language to describe what happened after each action.

docs.title: Helper role
docs.text: The helper gives clear commands, demonstrates as needed, and repeats with slight variation so the learner internalizes vocabulary and sentence patterns.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Gather 8–10 simple objects or picture cards.
  • Choose a safe space for movement.
  • Decide on the first set of action verbs and nouns.

docs.title: What TPR looks like
docs.text: The helper says a command such as "stand up," "point to the window," or "pick up the book." The learner performs the action without translating.

docs.title: Step 1: Start with single-word commands
docs.text: Use verbs and nouns together in simple sentences. The learner listens and moves, reinforcing meaning through motion.

docs.title: Step 2: Add objects and manipulatives
docs.text: Show the object or point to it while giving the command. This ties the spoken word to the physical referent.

docs.title: Step 3: Repeat and expand
docs.text: Repeat the same commands with slight variation. Add plural forms, new objects, and two-step directions.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Learners may want to ask for translation. Instead, the helper should restate the command more slowly and give a model action.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: The helper says, "Touch the red ball," then "Give the red ball to me." The learner follows both commands without stopping to translate.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Which command felt easiest to understand without translation?
  2. How did TPR change your confidence in hearing the target language?
  3. What object could you use next time to practice a new verb?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: TPR is a powerful early-stage method that teaches language by doing. It develops listening comprehension and vocabulary without reliance on English.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Plan a short TPR session with your helper that uses 8–10 objects or commands.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson overview
docs.text: The Picture Book Technique uses a binder of photos and repeated listening passes to build comprehension of nouns, verbs, and simple grammar.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Repeated exposure to the same culturally relevant images helps learners understand meaning gradually, without forcing translation.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: When learners see the same images again and again, they begin to recognize language patterns and meaning through context rather than through English.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This method works with local photographs of people, places, foods, and activities that are meaningful to the learner's host community.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner observes, listens, and responds to the helper's descriptions. They count repeated exposures and track the meanings they recognize.

docs.title: Helper role
docs.text: The helper describes the images in the target language, points to details, asks simple questions, and repeats the same images over multiple sessions.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Collect 70–100 photographs or images that reflect daily life in your host culture.
  • Put the images in a binder or notebook.
  • Label the images with simple target-language phrases if possible.

docs.title: What "Look and Listen" means
docs.text: The learner scans the picture while the helper speaks. The helper does not translate immediately but uses gestures, pointing, and repetition to build meaning.

docs.title: Step 1: Introduce the first 20 pictures
docs.text: Start with 20 photos and review them in a single session. The helper points, names, and asks yes/no or simple choice questions.

docs.title: Step 2: Return to the same pictures again
docs.text: In later sessions, use the same pictures. The learner will begin to recognize words in context and feel the language patterns.

docs.title: Step 3: Add grammar gradually
docs.text: Once nouns and verbs are familiar, the helper models simple plurals and sentence structures using the same images.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Learners may feel the images are too simple or slow. Remind them that repeated exposure is the key to internalizing the language.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A learner reviews a photo of a market three times with the helper. On the third pass, they can say, "The woman is buying tomatoes," using the target language.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Which picture felt easiest to understand without translation?
  2. What local object or activity should be added next?
  3. How will you use the same pictures over several days?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The Picture Book Technique turns everyday photos into language-building tools. It supports comprehension, vocabulary, and grammar without classroom drills.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Build or update your picture book with 20 new culturally relevant images and use it in the next session.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 04: Language Acquisition Projects (LAPs) & Shared Experiences
Lesson overview
docs.text: Language Acquisition Projects (LAPs) move learners from controlled practice into real community experiences with predictable vocabulary.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Prepare for a real task in advance, then execute it with simple language and local support.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Real-world language use builds confidence and shows learners how vocabulary and structures work in living contexts.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: LAPs are best when tied to real, shared activities such as shopping, cooking, or attending a local event.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner selects a project, prepares the necessary vocabulary, and practices the task with the helper before doing it in the community.

docs.title: Helper role
docs.text: The helper rehearses the scenario, corrects phrases, and then supports the learner during the real activity if needed.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Choose a realistic, culturally appropriate project.
  • List expected vocabulary and phrases.
  • Practice the task verbally and with gestures.

docs.title: What a LAP includes
docs.text: A LAP includes the situation, the people involved, the vocabulary needed, and a short post-task reflection.

docs.title: Step 1: Pick a predictable task
docs.text: Choose activities such as buying food, asking directions, ordering a meal, or joining a local workplace.

docs.title: Step 2: Learn the task vocabulary
docs.text: Focus on the words and sentences the learner is most likely to use. Keep them simple and functional.

docs.title: Step 3: Do the task with support
docs.text: Perform the LAP in the community, then review what worked and what needs more practice.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Learners may overprepare and then panic during the actual event. Use the LAP to rehearse the essentials and keep the task small.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A learner prepares vocabulary for a market purchase, rehearses the dialogue with the helper, and then completes the purchase using the target language.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What project seems most likely to help you speak in the target language?
  2. What vocabulary do you need to learn before the project?
  3. How will you review the project after it is finished?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: LAPs turn practice into purpose. They help learners connect language study with real ministry and daily life.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Select one LAP for the coming week and plan the vocabulary you need to complete it.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 05: The Emotional Toll of Language Learning
Lesson overview
docs.text: Language learning in the field is often an emotional assault on identity and self-worth. This lesson helps learners name the fatigue and pastoral care needs that arise.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Language learning can make competent adults feel like beginners. Teams must treat this struggle as a pastoral care issue, not merely a discipline issue.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: When workers dismiss emotional fatigue as weakness, they risk burnout, shame, and withdrawal from both people and practice.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This lesson applies to long-term learners, new arrivals, and anyone using a helper in a high-pressure environment.

docs.title: Learner role
docs.text: The learner listens to their feelings, shares discouragement honestly, and seeks support from mentors, teammates, or a helper.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Teams should normalize language frustration, provide encouragement, and connect learners with pastoral care or coaching.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Track energy levels and discouragement during language practice.
  • Identify trusted people who can speak gospel truth and practical encouragement.
  • Plan simple celebrations of small wins.

docs.title: What the emotional toll looks like
docs.text: Learners may feel embarrassed, frightened, angry, or incompetent when they cannot express basic needs.

docs.title: Step 1: Name the experience
docs.text: Help the learner describe how language practice makes them feel. Naming the emotion reduces isolation.

docs.title: Step 2: Reframe the process
docs.text: Remind learners that early language stages are normal. Everyone passes through a beginner identity before speaking with confidence.

docs.title: Step 3: Care for the whole person
docs.text: Use pastoral listening, rest, and spiritual encouragement alongside language practice. If needed, reduce the intensity of sessions temporarily.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Teams can mistakenly treat discouragement as a lack of discipline. Instead, they should validate the struggle and adjust expectations.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A learner meets with a teammate after a hard session. They review what went well, confess the hard feelings, and plan one encouraging next step.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What emotions surface when you struggle with language learning?
  2. Who can help you carry the weight of this process?
  3. How will you celebrate a small progress marker?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Language learning is both skill work and soul work. Healthy teams care for both the learner’s progress and their emotional stamina.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Discuss your current language rhythm with a mentor or teammate and identify one change that supports your emotional health.

Back Course Overview

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A quick worksheet for learners to translate 20 essential phrases with a Language Helper.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text: Translate each phrase into the target language, practice it aloud, and use it in the next helper session.

docs.title: Power tool phrases
docs.text:
1. Please repeat.
  2. Say it slower.
  3. Correct my mistake.
  4. Speak only in the target language.
  5. What does this mean?
  6. How do you say that?
  7. Can I try it again?
  8. Listen to me.
  9. I do not understand.
  10. I understand a little.
  11. Please write it down.
  12. Show me again.
  13. Is that right?
  14. Please ask me.
  15. Help me say this.
  16. Say it with me.
  17. One more time.
  18. What did you say?
  19. Please wait.
  20. Thank you.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use the list to keep sessions active and learner-centered.
  • Add additional phrases that are important for your context.
  • Review the list before every language practice meeting.

docs.title: Field Readiness
docs.text:
Field Readiness helps learners prepare for practical field entry with clear expectations, wise planning, and healthy communication.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course introduces practical questions learners should address before field involvement.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective missionaries and families.
  • Local churches and sponsors preparing workers.
  • Small organizations building basic field-entry expectations.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101, Missionary Care, and any required local or organizational training.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Identify practical questions that should be addressed before departure.
  • Prepare communication rhythms with sending partners.
  • Recognize basic risk, logistics, and transition concerns.
  • Clarify next steps with church, agency, or team leaders.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A field-readiness checklist and initial go-bag plan.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Preparing For Field Entry
  2. Environmental Awareness and Threat Levels
  3. Communication Protocols and Digital Hygiene
  4. Documents, Logistics, and Support Contacts
  5. Go-Bag Preparation and Evacuation Planning
  6. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete all five lessons.
  • Review field-specific requirements from your church, sponsor, organization, or local leaders.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. What practical uncertainty could create avoidable stress?
  2. What information do your sending partners need from you?
  3. Which field-specific questions require qualified guidance?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Create a field-readiness checklist covering documents, communication, support, health, risk, language learning, local expectations, and first-year priorities.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and all lessons.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft and review a field-readiness checklist with responsible leaders.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Do not give legal, immigration, medical, tax, or security advice unless qualified.
  • Encourage learners to identify the appropriate responsible person for each field-specific question.
  • Keep safety, family needs, and local accountability visible.

Caution

This course does not provide legal, immigration, security, tax, or medical advice. Consult qualified professionals and responsible leaders for field-specific decisions.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Preparing For Field Entry
docs.text: Field readiness brings practical questions into the open before avoidable stress becomes a crisis.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Good preparation helps learners clarify responsibilities, communicate expectations, and seek qualified guidance where needed.

docs.title: Documents And Requirements
docs.text: Workers may need passports, visas, permissions, insurance, emergency contacts, training records, or organization-specific documents.

docs.title: Communication
docs.text: Churches, sponsors, family, and team leaders need clear rhythms for updates, urgent concerns, and confidential matters.

docs.title: Health And Safety
docs.text: Health preparation, child protection, risk awareness, and emergency planning should be addressed with qualified leaders and professionals.

docs.title: First-Year Priorities
docs.text: The first year often requires language learning, relationship building, local orientation, and realistic limits.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. What field-entry topic do you understand least?
  2. Who can answer field-specific questions responsibly?
  3. What communication rhythm would help your supporters?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Field readiness is not about controlling every outcome. It is about preparing responsibly and knowing where wisdom, authority, and qualified help are needed.

docs.title: Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry
docs.text:
Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry is a Level 2 Field Practitioner course for workers who need practical tools for psychological care, multicultural team health, and transition resilience in cross-cultural service.

Field Practitioner course

This course is designed for active workers, tentmakers, and field teams who need resilient practices for person formation, team culture, and reentry.

docs.title: Course purpose
docs.text: This course teaches field practitioners how to build personal resilience, manage cultural distance, adapt to team subculture, care for single workers and families, and use the RAFT principle for healthy reentry.

docs.title: Intended audience
docs.text:
• Active cross-cultural workers and teams
  • Support leaders responsible for worker care
  • Individuals preparing for long-term service or furlough

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101, Field Readiness, or Missionary Care.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Describe why person formation is as important as missiological training
  • Assess cultural distance using the E-Scale
  • Adapt to both host culture and team culture
  • Support singles, families, and TCKs in diverse field settings
  • Use RAFT to plan healthy return or furlough transitions

docs.title: Estimated time
docs.text: 90-110 minutes.

docs.title: Practical output
docs.text: A resilience plan, E-Scale assessment, team covenant template, RAFT transition checklist, and a reentry debrief guide.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Person Formation and the Crucible of Cross-Cultural Stress
  2. The E-Scale of Ministry (Measuring Cultural Distance)
  3. Navigating Team Culture vs. Host Culture
  4. Single Workers, Marriages, and Third-Culture Kids (TCKs)
  5. Avoiding Reentry Shock: The RAFT Principle

docs.title: Field tools
docs.text:
• The RAFT Transition Checklist
  • Multicultural Team Covenant
  • The E-Scale Ministry Assessment
  • Reentry Debrief Guide

docs.title: Required reading/viewing
docs.text:
• Read this course overview.
  • Complete all five lessons and at least one tool.
  • Apply one resilience strategy in a real team or transition situation.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. When has cross-cultural stress revealed an old wound or family issue for you?
  2. Which E-Scale level fits your current calling?
  3. What does a healthy farewell look like in your context?

docs.title: Practical assignment
docs.text: Complete the E-Scale Ministry Assessment and the RAFT Transition Checklist for your next field assignment or furlough.

docs.title: Completion criteria
docs.text:
• Review all lessons and one tool page.
  • Use the E-Scale and RAFT templates in a real planning conversation.
  • Share your resilience plan with a mentor or support team.

docs.title: Facilitator notes
docs.text:
• Emphasize the importance of person formation and psychological care.
  • Encourage team agreements before field deployment.
  • Review reentry plans with experienced local and sending leaders.

docs.text: A worksheet to help independent workers define the cultural distance of their intended host community and assess preparation gaps.

docs.title: How to use this assessment
docs.text:
1. Place your calling on the E-Scale from E-0 to E-3.
  2. Describe the cultural distance and likely challenges.
  3. Identify preparation gaps in language, worldview, and support.

docs.title: Assessment template
docs.text: E-Scale level Description Your context Preparation needed E-0 Nominal Christians in own culture E-1 Unchurched people in same culture E-2 Similar but different culture E-3 Vastly different culture

docs.title: Lesson 1: Person Formation and the Crucible of Cross-Cultural Stress
Course/series
docs.text: Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field practitioners preparing for long-term or high-stress service
  • Workers who will live and minister in a culture far from home
  • Mentors supporting psychological and spiritual formation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners that cross-cultural stress exposes inner wounds, family-of-origin issues, and identity vulnerabilities, making person formation and inner healing essential before and during field service.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Identify how cross-cultural stress lowers self-image and reveals past trauma
  • Describe the role of person formation in missionary resilience
  • Name three signs that a worker needs deeper emotional support

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Healthy field ministry depends on the worker’s inner formation as much as theological knowledge.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers often enter the field with strong missiology but weak emotional grounding, leaving them vulnerable to burnout, conflict, or unhealthy coping when under stress.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Cross-cultural stress as a crucible
  • Person formation vs. information training
  • Hidden vulnerability from family-of-origin dynamics

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a resilience map to record stress triggers, past wounds, current coping habits, and support resources.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A new worker in a high-stress setting becomes reactive at small frustrations and discovers those reactions echo unresolved childhood shame.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• List current stressors in the host environment
  • Identify related emotional or relational patterns from your past
  • Note who can support you locally and remotely

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What early life pattern is most likely to resurface under stress?
  2. How is your self-image affected by cross-cultural stress?
  3. Who can you invite into your support network?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Create a personal resilience map and share it with a trusted mentor or peer.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• IWM resilience and psychological care frameworks
  • Passport to Mission guidance on worker formation

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Ensure the lesson language is compassionate and avoids stigmatizing vulnerability.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Lesson 2: The E-Scale of Ministry (Measuring Cultural Distance)
Course/series
docs.text: Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Workers planning cross-cultural service
  • Teams evaluating their adaptation needs
  • Mentors helping others gauge preparation gaps

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners to assess the cultural distance of their calling using the E-Scale, so they can match preparation to the demands of their assignment.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Define E-0 through E-3 ministry contexts
  • Place a calling on the E-Scale accurately
  • Identify preparation gaps based on E-Scale level

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: The greater the cultural distance, the higher the need for worldview adaptation, language learning, and support systems.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers often underestimate cultural distance, treating all ministry assignments as if they require the same level of preparation.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• E-0: evangelism in own culture among nominal Christians
  • E-1: outreach to unchurched people in same culture
  • E-2: ministry in a similar but different culture
  • E-3: ministry in a vastly different culture

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the E-Scale Ministry Assessment to place your calling and list specific preparation needs for language, culture, and worldview.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A worker assumes their early career skills are enough for a distant culture, then struggles with local assumptions and language nuance.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• What E-Scale number best describes your current calling?
  • Which areas of preparation are missing? (language, culture, community entry)
  • What support or training do you need next?

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. How does E-3 preparation differ from E-1 preparation?
  2. What assumptions might you need to let go of at your E-Scale level?
  3. Who can help you evaluate your cultural distance accurately?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Complete the E-Scale Ministry Assessment and compare your results with a mentor or sending team.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• E-Scale training from IWM and mission education literature
  • Cultural adaptation checklists for cross-cultural workers

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Verify that the E-Scale descriptions align with established mission education terminology.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Lesson 3: Navigating Team Culture vs. Host Culture
Course/series
docs.text: Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Missionary teams working across multiple cultures
  • Team leaders managing conflict and communication
  • Workers needing to adapt to their team subculture

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Help learners recognize that team culture can be as demanding as host culture, and teach them to adapt to their team’s norms of communication, conflict, and support.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Distinguish between team culture and host culture
  • Identify the conflict styles and communication norms of your team
  • Name one team adjustment that will reduce attrition

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: A healthy team culture is essential for field sustainability and often requires more adaptation than the host culture itself.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Missionaries may assume the host culture is the main source of stress, while ignoring friction from team norms, expectations, and subcultural dynamics.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Team subculture as a unique context
  • Conflict management expectations within the team
  • Communication habits and mutual adaptation

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a team covenant draft to record communication preferences, conflict responses, and support rhythms.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A multicultural team has different assumptions about feedback, causing repeated misunderstandings and early attrition.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• What are our team’s communication norms?
  • How do we handle conflict when it arises?
  • What support practices do we commit to as a team?

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. Which team habit feels most different from your home culture?
  2. How can you show respect for team norms while staying authentic?
  3. What behavior will you change to improve team health?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Draft a Multicultural Team Covenant with your team or discuss one with a trusted partner.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• Team culture and conflict resources from IWM
  • Missionary care literature on team dynamics

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Check that the team covenant model fits multicultural teams rather than assuming one cultural norm.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Lesson 4: Single Workers, Marriages, and Third-Culture Kids (TCKs)
Course/series
docs.text: Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Single workers and short-term volunteers
  • Married workers with children in the field
  • Leaders caring for TCKs and family wellbeing

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners to recognize the different stress patterns of single workers, married couples, and Third-Culture Kids, and to avoid judging one another’s responses.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Describe the unique challenges of single workers and career families
  • Identify key needs of TCKs in field settings
  • Name one practical support action for each demographic

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Different field demographics experience stress differently, and team care must honor those distinct needs without judgment.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Teams may assume all workers respond to stress the same way, which can harm singles, spouses, and TCKs by invalidating their experience.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Rapid culture shock and loneliness for short-term/volunteer workers
  • Competing demands on married workers and families
  • TCK formation and the need for intentional care

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a demographic support matrix to capture emotional needs, stress triggers, and practical care strategies for singles, couples, and children.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A team praises the resilience of a family while overlooking the burnout of a single worker who lacks community support.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• What support does the single worker need?
  • What are the family’s competing priorities?
  • How are the children being cared for emotionally and socially?

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What does healthy support look like for a single worker?
  2. How can families receive help without losing agency?
  3. What questions should we ask TCKs about their wellbeing?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Complete a demographic support matrix for your team, then discuss it with a leader or mentor.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• TCK care and family resilience resources
  • IWM research on family and psychological care in missions

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Ensure the lesson avoids overgeneralization and encourages individualized care.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes

docs.title: Lesson 5: Avoiding Reentry Shock: The RAFT Principle
Course/series
docs.text: Field Resilience, Team Health, and Reentry

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Returning workers preparing for furlough or home assignment
  • Support teams managing reentry care
  • Leaders planning healthy transitions for their people

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners the RAFT principle for reentry preparation: Reconciliation, Affirmation, Farewells, and Thinking Destination.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Explain the four RAFT components
  • Plan a healthy reentry process before leaving the host culture
  • Identify one potential source of reverse culture shock

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Safe reentry depends on intentional transition practices, not just physical travel home.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers often return or furlough without a plan, causing reverse culture shock, relational strain, and a sense of disorientation.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Reconciliation before departure
  • Affirmation for those who supported the worker
  • Intentional farewells that honor relationships
  • Realistic thinking about the next destination

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the RAFT Transition Checklist to plan concrete actions in each category before leaving the field.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A worker rushes home without farewells and returns to old routines without preparing for the emotional realities of reintegration.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Have I reconciled unresolved conflicts? yes/no
  • Have I affirmed key supporters? yes/no
  • Have I planned intentional farewells? yes/no
  • Have I thought through the next phase realistically? yes/no

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What relationships need reconciliation before you leave?
  2. Who should receive affirmation for their support?
  3. What do you expect to be most challenging when you return home?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Complete the RAFT Transition Checklist and review it with a mentor or sending team.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• IWM reentry and furlough care resources
  • Cross-cultural stress and reverse culture shock materials

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Make sure RAFT is presented as a practical tool, not legal or psychological counseling.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A tool for diverse teams to establish baseline rules for conflict management and communication before entering the field.

docs.title: How to use this covenant
docs.text:

docs.title: Lesson 01: Language Learning As Love
docs.text: Language learning is more than a practical skill. It is a way to honor people, listen carefully, and enter community with humility.

docs.title: Set Realistic Expectations
docs.text: Language learning takes time. Learners should expect embarrassment, fatigue, repeated mistakes, and slow progress.

docs.title: Listen First
docs.text: Listening builds pronunciation, rhythm, vocabulary, and cultural understanding. Early listening is not wasted time.

docs.title: Use Local Helpers

docs.title: Lesson 01: Care Is Part Of Readiness
docs.text: Missionary care is not a sign of weakness. It is part of responsible preparation for service that can involve stress, transition, conflict, loneliness, fatigue, or uncertainty.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Healthy service requires support before, during, and after field involvement.

docs.title: Name Likely Stressors
docs.text: Common stressors include culture shock, language fatigue, family strain, team conflict, financial pressure, loneliness, disappointment, and unclear expectations.

docs.title: Build Support Early
docs.text: Care is stronger when relationships already exist. Learners should identify pastors, mentors, sponsors, medical providers, counselors, and field contacts before a crisis.

docs.title: Communicate Clearly
docs.text: Supporters cannot respond well to vague or hidden needs. Learners should prepare honest communication rhythms that respect privacy and safety.

docs.title: Know When To Escalate
docs.text: Some issues require pastoral care, professional counseling, medical attention, child protection reporting, legal help, or security guidance.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:

docs.title: Lesson 01: What Missionary Readiness Means
docs.text: Missionary readiness is the sober preparation needed before serving across cultures, communities, or ministry contexts. It is not only a matter of desire. It includes formation, wisdom, support, humility, and practical responsibility.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Readiness is the process of becoming prepared to serve faithfully, safely, respectfully, and accountably.

docs.title: Spiritual Formation
docs.text: Missionary service should grow from worship, Scripture, prayer, obedience, and character. Skills matter, but character carries weight when plans change, language is difficult, relationships are strained, or ministry fruit is slow.

docs.title: Cross-Cultural Humility
docs.text: Missionaries enter communities as learners. Cultural respect includes listening carefully, honoring local leaders, avoiding superiority, and taking time to understand the people and place before acting.

docs.title: Accountability And Support
docs.text: Independent service should not mean isolated service. Healthy preparation includes trusted pastors, mentors, churches, sponsors, family conversations, and field relationships that can ask hard questions and provide care.

docs.title: Safety And Ethical Practice
docs.text: Readiness includes attention to family safety, child protection, legal compliance, appropriate boundaries, and responsible communication. Some concerns require qualified professional guidance and should not be handled casually.

docs.title: Practical Preparation
docs.text: Faithful service also requires ordinary planning: documents, finances, communication rhythms, language learning, health preparation, local expectations, and clear next steps.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, write brief answers to these questions:

  1. Which area of readiness is strongest for you right now?
  2. Which area needs the most attention?
  3. Who should be part of your readiness conversation?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Missionary readiness is not a badge or status. It is a posture and preparation process that helps workers serve with humility, faithfulness, accountability, and practical wisdom.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Mission Boundaries and Board Governance
Lesson overview
docs.text: Good intentions do not replace governance. This lesson helps organizations define theological and operational boundaries and use functional board meetings.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: A mission organization needs clear mission boundaries, bylaws, and documented board governance to stay accountable.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Without governance, mission drift, confusion, and harmful decisions become likely.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to small nonprofits, start-up ministries, and field-based organizations that need legal discipline.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should clarify mission boundaries and ensure the board holds the organization to them.

docs.title: Board role
docs.text: The board provides oversight, approves bylaws, and documents key decisions in minutes.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Draft a mission statement and organizational boundaries.
  • Review legal and theological parameters.
  • Prepare a board agenda template.

docs.title: Step 1: Define mission boundaries
docs.text: Document what the organization exists to do and what it will not do.

docs.title: Step 2: Establish bylaws
docs.text: Create bylaws that describe governance structure, membership, officer roles, and decision-making.

docs.title: Step 3: Hold functional board meetings
docs.text: Use agendas, minutes, and clear follow-up to make meetings effective and transparent.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Boards can become ceremonial if they do not have real authority or documented decisions.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A small ministry writes bylaws that define board composition, term limits, and a process for approving strategic goals.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What is one boundary your organization must state clearly?
  2. Who should serve on your board?
  3. How will you document board decisions?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Board governance and mission boundaries protect both the organization and those it serves.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Draft basic bylaws and a board meeting agenda for your next governance session.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not legal or financial advice. Consult a qualified attorney or governance specialist for bylaws and nonprofit structure.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 02: Ethical Conduct, Authority, and Conflicts of Interest
Lesson overview
docs.text: Ambiguous authority creates preventable harm. This lesson helps organizations establish conduct rules, authority lines, and conflict-of-interest protections.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Clear authority and ethical conduct policies keep boards and leaders accountable.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Without defined conduct and conflict policies, personal interests can override mission integrity.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to nonprofit boards, leadership teams, and field operations where authority and resources intersect.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should model ethical conduct and enforce conflict-of-interest disclosures.

docs.title: Board role
docs.text: The board should approve codes of conduct and review disclosures regularly.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Draft a code of conduct and conflict-of-interest policy.
  • Identify who reviews disclosures.
  • Create a whistleblower reporting process.

docs.title: Step 1: Define conduct expectations
docs.text: Establish clear behavior standards, reporting lines, and consequences.

docs.title: Step 2: Clarify authority
docs.text: Document who has final authority for decisions, approvals, and financial commitments.

docs.title: Step 3: Require disclosures
docs.text: Require board members and leaders to disclose conflicts annually and whenever they arise.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: People often assume shared values are enough. Policies are necessary to manage real-world pressures.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A board member discloses a family relationship with a vendor and recuses themselves from the approval process.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What authority lines are unclear in your organization?
  2. How will you document conflicts of interest?
  3. Who will receive whistleblower reports?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Ethical conduct and conflict policies are essential safeguards for healthy governance.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Draft a conflict-of-interest disclosure form and a basic code of conduct.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney or compliance professional for board governance and conflict-of-interest policies.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 03: Field Accounting and Cash Controls
Lesson overview
docs.text: Non-accountants need simple systems to manage field operations. This lesson explains how to separate personal and ministry funds, control petty cash, and document expenses.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Strong field accounting is practical, not complex. It depends on clear separation, documentation, and simple controls.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Mixing personal and ministry cash or failing to document expenses creates risk, confusion, and mistrust.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to cash-based field operations, local purchases, and low-banking environments.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should enforce separation of funds and require receipts for all ministry expenses.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Field workers should use the petty cash ledger, submit reimbursements, and keep receipts organized.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Create separate accounts or cash envelopes for ministry funds.
  • Prepare a petty cash ledger and reimbursement form.
  • Agree on approval policies for purchases.

docs.title: Step 1: Separate funds
docs.text: Keep personal funds separate from ministry funds at all times.

docs.title: Step 2: Control petty cash
docs.text: Use a ledger with columns for date, purpose, amount, payer, and approval.

docs.title: Step 3: Document expenses
docs.text: Require receipts or written explanations for every transaction.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Field staff may assume cash is informal. The right discipline is to treat cash with the same accountability as bank transfers.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A team member uses the petty cash ledger for a local purchase, attaches a receipt, and submits it for approval the same day.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. How will you separate personal and ministry cash in your context?
  2. What receipt controls will you enforce?
  3. Who approves expense reimbursements?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Simple accounting systems and cash controls provide transparency and protect the ministry.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Use the Petty Cash Ledger & Expense Reimbursement Form template for your next field purchase.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not financial advice. Consult a qualified accountant or finance professional for your context and legal requirements.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 04: Short-Term Volunteer Screening and Liability
Lesson overview
docs.text: Short-term workers create long-term risk if they are not screened, trained, supervised, and bound by safeguarding standards.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: Short-term volunteers require the same ethical screening and legal protections as career personnel.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Unscreened volunteers can expose the organization to liability, harm local partners, and undermine ministry credibility.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to visitors, teams, and short-term helpers entering sensitive ministry environments.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should require applications, references, background screening, and liability waivers.

docs.title: Team role
docs.text: Host teams should orient, supervise, and monitor short-term volunteers carefully.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Create a volunteer application form.
  • Draft a liability waiver and safeguarding agreement.
  • Define training and supervision expectations.

docs.title: Step 1: Screen applicants
docs.text: Collect pastoral references, relevant experience, and any required background checks.

docs.title: Step 2: Obtain liability waivers
docs.text: Use a waiver that is appropriate for your legal jurisdiction and describes expected risks.

docs.title: Step 3: Train and supervise
docs.text: Provide orientation, clear expectations, and active supervision for short-term workers.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Organizations sometimes assume volunteers are low risk. In reality, they often increase operational risk.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A ministry requires every short-term team member to submit an application and sign a liability waiver before arrival.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What screening steps are missing from your current process?
  2. Who reviews volunteer applications?
  3. How will you supervise short-term workers in the field?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Volunteer screening, training, and liability controls are essential to protect the organization and local communities.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Use the Short-Term Volunteer Application & Liability Waiver template for your next visitor.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for volunteer screening and liability issues.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 05: Annual Review and Document Retention
Lesson overview
docs.text: Oversight requires rhythm. This lesson helps organizations calendar annual budget review, goal-setting, and secure document retention.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text: If reporting is not calendared, it becomes reactive. Routine review and retention protect accountability.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Without an annual cycle, organizations miss deadlines, lose records, and become vulnerable to legal and financial risk.

docs.title: Field context
docs.text: This applies to budgets, board reviews, donor records, safeguarding files, and local legal documents.

docs.title: Leader role
docs.text: Leaders should establish a review calendar and ensure documents are stored securely.

docs.title: Board role
docs.text: The board should approve the annual review schedule and verify that retention policies are followed.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
• Create an annual calendar for budgets, goals, and reviews.
  • Identify critical documents and retention timelines.
  • Choose secure storage methods.

docs.title: Step 1: Calendar the year
docs.text: Schedule budgeting, reporting, compliance checks, and board review dates.

docs.title: Step 2: Define retention policies
docs.text: Decide how long to keep financial records, personnel files, and donor documents.

docs.title: Step 3: Secure documents
docs.text: Store documents physically or digitally in a secure, access-controlled location.

docs.title: Common challenges
docs.text: Records are often kept haphazardly until a review or audit forces cleanup.

docs.title: Practical example
docs.text: A ministry creates a calendar with quarterly financial reviews, an annual board meeting, and a five-year record retention schedule.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What important deadlines are missing from your current schedule?
  2. Where will you store sensitive documents securely?
  3. How long should you keep ministry and financial records?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: A disciplined annual review and retention schedule keeps oversight proactive and defensible.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text:
Create an annual review calendar and add document retention tasks to your next board agenda.

Risk / Disclaimer: This lesson is for general training only and is not legal or financial advice. Consult a qualified attorney or accountant for your organization’s document retention requirements.

Back Course Overview

docs.title: Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship
docs.text:
Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship is a Level 2 Field Practitioner course for workers who need practical, oral-first ministry tools for church planting and discipleship in oral or semi-oral communities.

Field Practitioner course

This course is designed for active field workers, tentmakers, and small teams who rely on storying, listening, and movement-based learning.

docs.title: Course purpose
docs.text: This course teaches field teams how to use Chronological Bible Storying, S-T4T evangelism flow, Person of Peace networks, and Disciple-Making Learning Communities in oral and movement-based ministry.

docs.title: Intended audience
docs.text:
• Field practitioners working in oral or secondarily oral communities
  • Tentmakers and neighborhood workers seeking movement-based discipleship
  • Mentors and trainers equipping Bible storying and DMLC teams

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101, Cross-Cultural Service, or Community & Worldview Fieldbook.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Explain why literate teaching methods often fail in oral cultures and secondary orality contexts
  • Use Chronological Bible Storying to communicate the Gospel as an intact narrative
  • Follow the S-T4T evangelism flow from listening to commitment
  • Identify Persons of Peace and build relationships through tentmaking influence
  • Launch a Disciple-Making Learning Community with a 3:1 practice-to-theory ratio

docs.title: Estimated time
docs.text: 90-120 minutes.

docs.title: Practical output
docs.text: A completed storying plan, evangelism flowchart, person of peace map, and 90-day DMLC action plan.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. The Orality Challenge and Secondary Orality
  2. Chronological Bible Storying (CBS)
  3. The S-T4T Evangelism Flow
  4. Finding the Person of Peace & Tentmaking
  5. Disciple-Making Learning Communities (DMLC)

docs.title: Field tools
docs.text:
• The 10-Step Story Crafting Worksheet
  • S-T4T Evangelism Flowchart
  • Person of Peace Community Mapping Guide
  • DMLC 90-Day Action Plan

docs.title: Required reading/viewing
docs.text:
• Read this course overview.
  • Complete all five lessons and one field tool.
  • Practice one oral story with a local listener and one person of peace conversation.

docs.title: Reflection questions
docs.text:
1. What learning methods do locals prefer in your current context?
  2. What part of oral storying feels most different from your usual teaching?
  3. Who could be your first Person of Peace in the next 30 days?

docs.title: Practical assignment
docs.text: Create a storying plan, use the S-T4T flow with one listener, and develop a DMLC goal for the next 90 days.

docs.title: Completion criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and all five lessons.
  • Use at least one field tool in a real ministry setting.
  • Complete the reflection questions and action plan.
  • Share your storying notes with a mentor or local partner.

docs.title: Facilitator notes
docs.text:
• Emphasize listening and local discovery over explanation.
  • Keep the training practical and movement-oriented.
  • Check the story selections for cultural relevance and clarity.
