docs.title: Lesson 1: The Orality Challenge and Secondary Orality
Course/series
docs.text: Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers serving oral and secondarily oral communities
  • Trainers shifting from literate teaching to story-based ministry
  • Teams preparing movement-based discipleship pathways

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Show why literate, exposition-based methods often fail in oral settings and why secondary orality makes narrative ministry essential in both rural and digitally connected populations.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Define oral and secondary orality in ministry contexts
  • Identify three ways literate methods hinder Gospel penetration
  • Recognize when narrative ministry is needed over outline-based teaching

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Ministry that follows the listener’s preferred way of learning is more likely to penetrate worldview and reproduce disciples.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Western workers often default to outlines, logic, and print-based teaching, which can leave oral listeners disengaged and resistant to the gospel.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Oral culture learning by story and example
  • Secondary orality driven by screens, video, and social media
  • The mismatch between Western exposition and oral worldview processing

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a brief assessment: identify the audience’s learning preference, note the current method, and choose an oral alternative.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A worker prepares an analytical sermon with points and scripture references for a group that prefers story, memory, and relational dialogue.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Does the audience prefer stories, sound, and memory? yes/no
  • Is the teaching method outline-based or narrative-based?
  • What oral alternative could better carry the Gospel?

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. When has a story engaged someone more deeply than a lecture?
  2. How does secondary orality show up in your context?
  3. What is one teaching habit you can change this week?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Choose one Gospel truth and tell it as a single, intact story to at least one local listener. Record how they respond and what language choices worked.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• Making Disciples of Oral Learners
  • S-T4T field notes on oral evangelism

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Check that the terminology for secondary orality matches local observations and that the story example is culturally appropriate.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Course/series
docs.text: Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers using oral methods for Gospel communication
  • Trainers teaching storying for discipleship
  • Teams learning to deliver intact narratives with clear application

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners to use Chronological Bible Storying to change worldviews without triggering cultural defenses, by telling the story as an intact narrative and using post-story discovery.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Describe the difference between intact, interpreted, and interrupted narrative
  • Explain why post-story dialogue is essential for discovery discipleship
  • Choose a story sequence that flows from creation to church

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Storying that preserves narrative integrity and invites discovery yields deeper spiritual understanding than didactic explanation.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Many workers stop the story to explain points, which makes listeners depend on the teacher’s interpretation instead of discovering truth themselves.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Intact narrative: the full story told without interruption
  • Interpreted narrative: teaching about the story instead of telling it
  • Interrupted narrative: breaking the story into points as it is told

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use a story plan with three columns: story selection, narrative cues, and discovery questions for after the story.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A worker tells parts of the Rich Young Ruler story, stops for each application, and never allows the listener to experience the narrative arc.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Is the story told completely from beginning to end? yes/no
  • Are teaching points held until after the story is finished? yes/no
  • What question will invite discovery after the story?

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. How does an intact story feel different from a lesson?
  2. What local story could help people discover the Gospel?
  3. How will you avoid interrupting the narrative?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Prepare one chronological Bible story and tell it intact. Then ask a discovery question that leads the listener toward the Gospel.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• Chronological Bible Storying guides
  • Oral discovery questions for story-based ministry

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Validate the story plan with experienced oral communicators and avoid using foreign cultural images in the narrative.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Course/series
docs.text: Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers using story-based evangelism
  • Trainers preparing others for movement-based ministry
  • Teams needing a simple flow for oral Gospel sharing

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach the S-T4T evangelism flow that moves from listening to testimony, story, Gospel bridge, and commitment in a reproducible pattern.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Recall the four steps of the S-T4T evangelism flow
  • Use their own story and a hook story to invite spiritual response
  • Apply the flow in a local conversation with a Person of Peace pattern

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: The Gospel advances most naturally through listening, testimony, story, and invitation rather than through impersonal presentation.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Some workers treat evangelism as a one-way presentation rather than a relational flow that honors local stories and invites response.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Listen to their cultural story
  • Tell Your Story (personal testimony)
  • Tell a Hook story and invite change
  • Tell Creation to Church and ask for commitment

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the S-T4T flowchart to plan each step, including listening questions, testimony points, hook story selection, and Gospel invitation.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A worker jumps immediately to a Gospel message before asking about the listener’s story, losing the chance to connect with local experience.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Have I listened to the person’s story first? yes/no
  • Did I tell a personal testimony? yes/no
  • Is my hook story relevant and understood locally? yes/no
  • Did I ask for commitment after the Creation to Church story? yes/no

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What local story could serve as your hook story?
  2. How will you tell your own faith story briefly and clearly?
  3. Who might be willing to hear the Gospel through this flow?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Use the S-T4T evangelism flow with one person. Start by listening, then tell your story, a hook story, and the Creation to Church story with a call to commitment.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• S-T4T evangelism materials
  • Mark 5 and other hook-story examples

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Review the chosen hook story and creation-to-church sequence with a mentor to ensure it is both oral and culturally accessible.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Lesson 4: Finding the Person of Peace & Tentmaking
Course/series
docs.text: Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Church planters seeking receptive relationships
  • Tentmakers building Gospel influence through work
  • Teams using Luke 10 principles in the field

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners to identify and follow a Person of Peace instead of forcing the Gospel on resistant communities, and to leverage tentmaking relationships for natural access and trust.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Define the Person of Peace pattern from Luke 10
  • Identify signs of a locally prepared heart
  • Map tentmaking relationships that build influence and access

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: The Gospel advances most sustainably through people God has already prepared, not through pressure on closed communities.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Workers who push the gospel on every contact often face rejection and persecution, while missing the Person of Peace who opens a whole network.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Person of Peace: an open social bridge
  • Luke 10 pattern of welcome and blessing
  • Tentmaking: relationship-building through secular work

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the Person of Peace Community Mapping Guide to chart relationships, daily contacts, and potential peace-people in your workplace or neighborhood.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise
docs.text: A tentmaker notices a market vendor who listens closely and asks about faith, then deliberately follows that relationship rather than preaching broadly.

docs.title: Checklist or worksheet
docs.text:
• Who in your network shows openness to the Gospel?
  • What relationships are built through your work or daily rhythm?
  • How can you pray specifically for a Person of Peace?

docs.title: Discussion questions
docs.text:
1. What signs indicate a Person of Peace in your context?
  2. How does your work help you access new relationships?
  3. What will you do this week to build trust with one priority contact?

docs.title: Field assignment
docs.text: Map your workplace or neighborhood relationships and identify at least one Person of Peace candidate and one pathway to build trust.

docs.title: Further reading/resources
docs.text:
• Luke 10 Person of Peace training notes
  • Tentmaking examples from movement-based ministry

docs.title: Reviewer notes
docs.text: Validate the mapped Person of Peace candidates with a mentor and make sure the relationships are grounded in respect and integrity.

docs.title: Risk/disclaimer notes
docs.text: This material is for educational purposes and is not legal, medical, tax, accounting, counseling, or security advice. Consult qualified professionals before adopting policies or making high-risk decisions.

docs.title: Course/series
docs.text: Orality & Movement-Based Discipleship

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
• Field teams committed to sustained movement building
  • Trainers leading action-oriented learning groups
  • Leaders needing accountability systems for disciple-making

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Teach learners how to form and sustain Disciple-Making Learning Communities that prioritize practice over theory, experimentation over isolation, and results over good intentions.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
• Describe the DMLC 3:1 practice-to-theory ratio
  • Identify key roles and rhythms for a DMLC
  • Create a 90-day action plan for field learning and accountability

docs.title: Core principle
docs.text: Sustainable movements grow when teams learn together in the field with more practice than theory.

docs.title: Field problem
docs.text: Isolated workers often plan without testing, learn without accountability, and fail to reproduce because they lack a community of practice.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Practice before theory (3 parts practice, 1 part theory)
  • Continuous experimentation and rapid feedback
  • Accountability based on tangible fruit

docs.title: Practical framework
docs.text: Use the DMLC 90-Day Action Plan to set SMART goals, practice cycles, review meetings, and accountability markers.

docs.title: Scenario or case exercise

docs.title: Person of Peace Community Mapping Guide
Purpose
docs.text: Help tentmakers and field workers map local networks and identify potential Persons of Peace in their workplace or neighborhood.

docs.title: How to use this guide
docs.text:
1. List your daily contacts through work, church, market, and social life.
  2. Note who is open, who listens, and who influences others.
  3. Identify the people most likely to welcome the Gospel and open their network.

docs.title: Mapping template
docs.text: Contact Relationship Openness Influence Potential as Person of Peace

docs.title: Questions to ask
docs.text:
• Who shows interest in spiritual matters?
  • Who invites conversation or offers hospitality?
  • Who others trust and follow?

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Tentmakers often find Persons of Peace through ordinary work relationships.
  • Respect each contact and avoid manipulating relationships.
  • Pray for guidance before approaching potential Persons of Peace.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A step-by-step guide for moving from listening to sharing the Possessed Man story and the Creation to Church panorama in S-T4T evangelism.

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Listen to their cultural story.
  2. Tell Your Story (personal testimony).
  3. Tell a Hook story (e.g., the Possessed Man in Mark 5). Ask: "Do you want to be changed like this person?"
  4. If yes, tell the Creation to Church story.
  5. Ask for commitment and next steps.

docs.title: Flowchart
docs.text: Step Action Key question 1 Listen to their story What does this story say about their world? 2 Tell Your Story How has Jesus changed you? 3 Tell Hook story Do you want to be changed like him/her? 4 Tell Creation to Church Will you follow Jesus now? 5 Follow-up Who will walk with you?

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Keep each step short and oral.
  • Make the hook story simple, vivid, and locally relevant.
  • Use the Creation to Church story as the Gospel climax.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: A field tool for crafting a Bible story that is culturally relevant, narrative-driven, and ready for oral delivery.

docs.title: How to use this worksheet
docs.text:
1. Identify the principle or Gospel truth.
  2. Evaluate worldview bridges and barriers.
  3. Select a story that fits the local context.
  4. Craft the story in simple, oral language.
  5. Communicate the story as an intact narrative.
  6. Apply the story through dialogue.
  7. Invite obedience based on the story.
  8. Build accountability into the follow-up.
  9. Reproduce the story with others.
  10. Review and adapt for the next listener.

docs.title: Worksheet
docs.text: Step Task Notes 1 Identify principle 2 Evaluate worldview bridges/barriers 3 Select story 4 Craft story 5 Communicate story 6 Apply/dialogue 7 Obey 8 Accountability 9 Reproduce 10 Review/adapt

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Keep the story simple and memorable.
  • Use local images and examples when possible.
  • Test the story with a trusted listener before wider use.

docs.title: Spiritual Formation
docs.text:
Spiritual Formation prepares learners for missionary service rooted in worship, Scripture, prayer, obedience, and durable love for God and neighbor.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course helps learners examine the spiritual practices, character concerns, and accountability relationships needed for faithful missionary service.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective missionaries.
  • Church members preparing for cross-cultural service.
  • Mentors, pastors, and sponsors discussing readiness.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Describe why spiritual formation is central to readiness.
  • Identify rhythms that sustain prayer, Scripture, worship, and rest.
  • Recognize pressures that can expose unhealthy expectations.
  • Discuss spiritual readiness honestly with trusted leaders.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A simple weekly rule-of-life and formation plan.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Formation Before Activity
  2. Worship and Scripture Rhythms
  3. Prayer, Rest, and Emotional Resilience
  4. Accountability, Community, and Confession
  5. Obedience Habits and Next Steps
  6. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete all five lessons.
  • Read a selected Scripture passage on character, prayer, or endurance.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. Where are you tempted to measure readiness by activity rather than faithfulness?
  2. Which spiritual rhythm most needs attention before service?
  3. Who can ask you honest questions about your character and practices?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Draft a simple weekly rule of life for the next month. Include Scripture, prayer, worship, rest, service, and one accountability conversation.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and all lessons.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft and discuss the practical assignment with a mentor, pastor, or trusted leader.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Keep the discussion concrete and personal without forcing disclosure.
  • Watch for signs that a learner needs pastoral care or professional support.
  • Encourage durable practices rather than short-term intensity.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Formation Before Activity
docs.text: Missionary service can expose the difference between visible activity and quiet faithfulness. Spiritual formation is the ongoing work of being shaped by God through Scripture, prayer, worship, obedience, repentance, rest, and community.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Missionary readiness begins with the kind of person a learner is becoming, not only with the tasks a learner hopes to perform.

docs.title: Scripture And Prayer
docs.text: Workers need regular habits of listening to God before speaking for God. Scripture and prayer provide stability when culture, language, relationships, or plans feel unfamiliar.

docs.title: Worship And Obedience
docs.text: Readiness includes love for God expressed in ordinary obedience. Public ministry should not outrun private faithfulness.

docs.title: Rest And Limits
docs.text: Mission work can reward urgency. Learners should prepare to receive limits, practice rest, and resist measuring worth by productivity alone.

docs.title: Accountability
docs.text: Trusted leaders and peers can help learners notice blind spots, confess honestly, and stay teachable.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. Which practice is currently strongest?
  2. Which practice is most neglected?
  3. What would a sustainable next step look like this week?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Spiritual formation is not a decorative add-on to missionary service. It is part of the foundation for faithful, humble, and durable ministry.

Back Next Lesson

docs.text: Spiritual formation is built on habits, not bursts of activity before departure. Scripture and worship should shape daily life.

docs.title: Team Dynamics, Leadership, and Conflict Repair
docs.text:
Team Dynamics, Leadership, and Conflict Repair equips teams to manage high-stakes interpersonal issues, prevent burnout, and restore trust using proven frameworks.

Level 2 course

This course is designed for team leaders, field practitioners, and support partners who want healthier team culture.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: To help teams build resilient, decentralized leadership; navigate difficult conversations; repair conflict; and share burdens in healthy ways.

docs.title: Intended Audience

  • Describe decentralized, starfish-style leadership.
  • Use Crucial Conversations principles to handle high-stakes dialogue.
  • Recognize self-deception and shift to people-focused thinking.
  • Build team rhythms for shared burden and debriefing.

docs.title: Worldview Transformation & Avoiding Syncretism
docs.text:
Worldview Transformation & Avoiding Syncretism is a Level 2 course for workers and local leaders who need a practical framework for identifying hidden cultural assumptions and guarding against syncretism.

Worldview course

This course is intended for learners already familiar with basic cross-cultural service and missionary readiness.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course equips learners to see how deeply held worldviews shape behavior, to recognize the signs of syncretism, and to hold Christian practice to a gospel-centered standard.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Field workers and team members in oral or animistic settings.
  • Local leaders and church planters seeking worldview clarity.
  • Mentors and sending partners helping learners avoid syncretism.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Cross-Cultural Service, Culture & Worldview Fieldbook, or equivalent cultural learning experience.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Name the basic features of a worldview and the role of the three cultural buckets.
  • Explain how syncretism and dual allegiance differ.
  • Use the first and second circle model to assess how assumptions shape behavior.
  • Detect when Christian tools are being used like cultural charms or bargaining devices.
  • Describe how gospel focus must replace cultural foci for lasting transformation.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Practical Output
docs.text: A course reflection that maps one local practice, reports the worldview assumptions behind it, and explains how the gospel keeps the practice faithful without imposing unrelated cultural rules.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Lesson 01: The Three Buckets Framework
  2. Lesson 02: Syncretism vs Dual Allegiance
  3. Lesson 03: The Cycles of Behavior
  4. Lesson 04: Tool Swaps and Player Confusion
  5. Lesson 05: Replacing Core Foci
  6. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Suggested Reading & Resources
docs.text:
• Ministering in Honor-Shame Cultures — culture, honor, and gospel communication.
  • Peace Child — a classic story of culture, conversion, and worldview shift.
  • The 3D Gospel — a whole-person gospel that addresses survival, social identity, and spiritual need.
  • The Culture Map — practical habits for cultural understanding and communication.
  • Cross-Cultural Servanthood — building humble service across cultural boundaries.
  • Anthropological Insights for Missionaries — worldviews, symbols, and local belief systems.
  • Making Disciples of Oral Learners — oral discipleship and story-based teaching.
  • Truth That Sticks — training messages that stick in non-literate settings.
  • The Art of Storytelling — using narrative to teach gospel truth.
  • Funding Your Ministry — ethical donor engagement for field work.
  • The God Ask — faithful asking for ministry support.
  • People Raising — building relationships for sustainable support.
  • Nonprofit Bookkeeping & Accounting for Dummies — simple systems for financial accountability.
  • Serving with Eyes Wide Open — cultural intelligence and risk awareness.
  • 100 Deadly Skills — practical safety habits for uncertain field environments.
  • Extreme Ownership — leadership responsibility for team and mission integrity.
  • The Gift of Fear — trusting wise instincts for personal and team safety.
  • Crucial Conversations — healthy team communication under pressure.
  • The Starfish and the Spider — resilient leadership and distributed team structures.

  3. What first question will you ask before introducing a Christian practice locally?

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Every culture organizes behavior and belief into three buckets: required actions, prohibited actions, and neutral choices.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Learn the three buckets framework and why preserving the middle bucket protects new believers from legalism.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• The Top Bucket contains required actions, duties, and identity markers.
  • The Bottom Bucket contains forbidden actions and clear moral boundaries.
  • The Middle Bucket contains neutral or optional practices shaped by culture, context, and personal choice.
  • Legalism happens when neutral cultural preferences are forced into the Top or Bottom buckets.
  • The Middle Bucket is a classroom of grace where local believers can learn how to apply biblical principles without unnecessary cultural burden.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Select one local practice. Decide whether it belongs in the Top, Bottom, or Middle bucket, and explain why. Identify one Middle Bucket practice that should remain optional rather than morally required.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: • Clothes style: Middle Bucket if it is a cultural preference, Top Bucket if it is required by local religious law, Bottom Bucket if it is clearly prohibited by Christian ethics.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Use this lesson to protect the gospel from legalism by keeping cultural preferences in the Middle Bucket until local believers choose them freely.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Learn the difference between blending opposing spiritual systems and keeping two systems separate.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Clarify syncretism and dual allegiance so you can help believers pursue one gospel-based way of life.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• Syncretism is the blending of Christianity with an opposing spiritual paradigm.
  • Dual Allegiance is holding two systems separately, using each when it suits the situation.
  • The Two Cups image helps show the danger of switching between faith systems.
  • Both problems arise when the gospel is taught as abstract belief instead of a full operating system for life.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Choose one local practice or belief and describe: - How it could become syncretism if it is mixed into Christian worship. - How it could become dual allegiance if it is used in a separate way when fear or crisis returns.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: • A believer prays in church but goes to a sorcerer when a child is sick. That is dual allegiance, not faithful gospel transformation.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Use this lesson to help local believers choose one consistent gospel system, not a mixture of Christian words and traditional spiritual practices.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Human behavior is shaped by a repeating cycle of experience, assumptions, perception, and response.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Use the first and second circle model to see how worldview change must move beyond facts to new assumptions.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• The cycle begins with past life experience.
  • Experience builds worldview assumptions.
  • Assumptions shape how people perceive current circumstances.
  • Perception triggers emotional and behavioral responses.
  • The first circle is the traditional cultural cycle.
  • The second circle is the Christ-centered cycle that discipleship seeks to establish.
  • Without a second circle, old assumptions continue to intercept and interpret reality.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Pick one local belief or practice and map it through the cycle: - What past experience created the assumption? - How does that assumption shape current interpretation? - What behavior does that lead to? - How would a Christ-centered second circle change the assumption and response?

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• Assumption: sickness is caused by angry spirits.
  • Circumstance: a child is ill.
  • Response: fear and ritual sacrifice.
  • Second circle: Bible teaches God as healer and comfort, changing the response to prayer, care, and community support.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Use this lesson to guide local discipleship toward a Christ-centered cycle, not simply more Bible facts.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Lesson 04: Tool Swaps and Player Confusion
Key Idea
docs.text: Local people use tools and players from their worldview to manage fear and spiritual danger. Christian tools can be misused if the underlying logic is not changed.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Learn to spot tool swaps and player confusion so gospel practice remains distinct from cultural spiritual systems.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• In animistic contexts, charms, magic words, and sacrifices are tools for protection or favor.
  • A tool swap happens when a believer drops traditional charms but uses Christian tools with the same manipulative motive.
  • Player confusion happens when biblical figures are mapped onto local spirit-world roles.
  • Missionaries should map local players and tools and explicitly compare them with biblical roles.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: List one local spiritual tool and one Christian practice. Describe how the same motive could be behind both, and how you would teach the difference.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• Local tool: a protective charm worn to avoid sickness.
  • Christian practice: prayer for healing.
  • If prayer is used as a way to manipulate God or spirits, it becomes a tool swap.
  • Instead, teach prayer as relationship, dependence, and obedience.

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text:
Use this lesson to keep Christian practices connected to biblical players and motives, not local spirit-world mechanics.

Back Next Lesson

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Local faith systems are organized around a central focus. The gospel must replace that focus rather than become a new tool for achieving it.

docs.title: Lesson Goal
docs.text: Learn how to identify cultural core foci and how to help believers redirect them toward gospel priorities.

docs.title: What This Lesson Teaches
docs.text:
• A culture’s core focus can be survival, merit, status, comfort, or spiritual protection.
  • If Christianity does not replace that core focus, it becomes another system for achieving the same ends.
  • A Western missionary may unintentionally model a core focus of comfort or security.
  • A local believer may adopt Christianity as a way to gain merit, wealth, or social advantage.
  • Lasting transformation happens when the gospel core focus of reconciliation, obedience, and Kingdom witness becomes primary.

docs.title: Practical Action
docs.text: Identify one cultural core focus in your current context. Describe: - What the culture is trying to achieve through that focus. - How a gospel-centered focus would redirect that energy. - One way to model the gospel core focus in teaching or practice.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:

docs.title: Worldview & Tool Swap Assessment
docs.text: Use this assessment as a downloadable course resource. Complete it with a mentor or field leader and save it as part of your practical output.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text:
1. Review the Worldview Transformation course lessons.
  2. Choose one Christian practice or ritual, such as prayer, communion, tithing, spiritual leadership, or storytelling.
  3. Reflect on the local worldview that surrounds that practice.
  4. Answer the questions below in writing.

docs.title: Part 1: Context
docs.text:
• Country/region/context:
  • Local spiritual assumptions or beliefs:
  • Primary local coping strategy (e.g. protection, merit, status, comfort):

docs.title: Part 2: The Christian Practice
docs.text:
• Christian practice selected:
  • Typical purpose in Christian teaching:
  • How the practice is often described to new believers:

docs.title: Part 3: Local Interpretation
docs.text:
• How might a local believer interpret this practice from their current worldview?
  • What local players or spiritual forces could be associated with the practice?
  • What would the practice look like if it became a tool swap?

docs.title: Part 4: Gospel Clarification
docs.text:
• What is the biblical purpose of the practice?
  • How can you teach the difference between a Christian tool and a local spiritual tool?
  • What questions will you use to help the learner see the gospel focus instead of a cultural focus?

docs.title: Part 5: Reflection
docs.text:
• What is the most important difference between the Christian practice and the local spiritual tool?
  • What is one action you can take to keep the gospel distinct while still honoring local culture?

Submit your completed assessment to your mentor or keep it in your course notebook as evidence of your learning.

docs.title: Policies
docs.text:
This section gathers public policy and compliance pages for the missionary readiness platform.

Policy draft

These policy pages are public drafts for a small nonprofit-style educational project. They should be reviewed before relying on them as final legal documents.

The site is currently static and hosted on GitHub Pages. It does not currently collect user accounts directly, process payments directly, or claim accreditation. External tools such as Google Forms, email, GitHub Pages, or analytics may be used later and should be disclosed when added.

Policy pages should be treated as starter templates and reviewed carefully before formal launch or wider use.

docs.text:

docs.title: Review Priorities
docs.text:
• Keep contact information and effective dates current.
  • Confirm the responsible legal or ministry organization.
  • Confirm the final content and code license.
  • Avoid unsupported claims about accreditation, legal compliance, safety procedures, or professional services.
  • Update policies before collecting personal information, using external forms, enabling analytics, accepting payments, or issuing certificates of completion.

docs.title: Acceptable Use
docs.text:
Effective date: June 7, 2026

Policy draft

This acceptable use policy is public draft text for a small nonprofit-style educational project. It should be reviewed before relying on it as a final legal document.

This site exists to support practical missionary readiness training. Visitors are expected to use the site in a lawful, respectful, and responsible way.

docs.title: Appropriate Use
docs.text:
You may use this site to:

  • Read and share public training content.
  • Adapt eligible materials according to the stated license.
  • Contact the project with questions, corrections, or partnership inquiries.
  • Use course content for personal, church, family, sponsor, or small-organization preparation.

docs.title: Prohibited Use
docs.text:
Do not use this site to:

  • Harass, threaten, exploit, or harm others.
  • Submit unlawful, abusive, misleading, or malicious content.
  • Attempt to disrupt, scan, attack, or compromise the site or its hosting providers.
  • Misrepresent the platform as an accredited institution or degree-granting program.
  • Claim that course participation certifies, appoints, deploys, or professionally qualifies someone for missionary service.
  • Remove attribution, imply endorsement, or present site content as your own.
  • Use the content in ways that disrespect local churches, local leaders, family safety, child protection, legal compliance, or ethical mission practice.

docs.title: Sensitive Information
docs.text:
Do not submit sensitive personal information through general contact forms, email links, public issue trackers, or other general communication channels connected to this site.

Sensitive information includes medical, counseling, legal, immigration, tax, financial, child protection, field security, crisis, or confidential pastoral details.

docs.title: External Tools
docs.text: The site may later link to external tools such as Google Forms, email services, analytics, or course platforms. Use those tools according to their own terms and privacy policies.

docs.title: Reporting Concerns
docs.text:
Concerns about misuse, errors, or unsafe content may be sent to robert@tetra6.com.

For urgent safety, legal, medical, child protection, or security matters, contact the appropriate local authority, qualified professional, church leader, or organization-specific reporting process.

docs.text: Mission work varies widely by country, culture, legal setting, family situation, church relationship, and security environment.
